Includes MCP docs (already validated).\\nQueries:\\n  \\u2022 Model Context Protocol MCP adoption study\\n  \\u2022 tool use LLM error rate latency benchmark\\n  \\u2022 function calling productivity benchmark enterprise\\n  \\u2022 external tool integration LLM workflow efficiency\\nFalsification target: friction cost, latency, or error rate of MCP exceeds productivity gain.\\n\\n\\u2550\\u2550\\u2550 C6 \\u2014 Operational efficiency + rework reduction \\u2550\\u2550\\u2550 CRITICAL\\nMin: 10 sources. This is the WEAKEST link in the thesis.\\nQueries:\\n  \\u2022 Workday Hanover Research AI rework 40% time saved\\n  \\u2022 KPMG University of Melbourne AI work errors 48340 study\\n  \\u2022 Microsoft Work Trend Index 2024 2025 2026\\n  \\u2022 Anthropic AI Fluency Index 9830 conversations iteration\\n  \\u2022 generative AI productivity rework reduction peer-reviewed\\n  \\u2022 LLM output quality verification rework cost\\nFalsification target: studies showing AI increases total rework, generates false productivity, or where time saved is offset by correction. ATTENTION: distinguish statistics about the PROBLEM (unstructured use) from statistics about the INTERVENTION EFFECT (structured use).\\n\\n\\u2550\\u2550\\u2550 C7 \\u2014 Onboarding and reusable workflows \\u2550\\u2550\\u2550\\nMin: 4 sources.\\nQueries:\\n  \\u2022 knowledge worker onboarding ROI study\\n  \\u2022 Building Second Brain GTD productivity peer-reviewed\\n  \\u2022 reusable workflow corporate training effectiveness\\n  \\u2022 playbook documentation team productivity meta-analysis\\nFalsification target: structured onboarding does not correlate with durable productivity or produces system fatigue.\\n\\n\\u2550\\u2550\\u2550 C8 \\u2014 Cross-platform content repurposing \\u2550\\u2550\\u2550\\nMin: 4 sources.\\nQueries:\\n  \\u2022 cross-media content repurposing strategy peer-reviewed\\n  \\u2022 content modularization multi-platform marketing\\n  \\u2022 one-source many-channel content reuse academic\\nFalsification target: repurposing dilutes perceived quality of primary channel.\\n\\n\\u2550\\u2550\\u2550 C9 \\u2014 Brazilian focus \\u2550\\u2550\\u2550\\nMin: 5 sources. Queries in Portuguese.\\nQueries:\\n  \\u2022 SBC ASAG prompt engineering portugu\\u00eas Brasil\\n  \\u2022 SciELO IA generativa letramento educa\\u00e7\\u00e3o Brasil\\n  \\u2022 IA generativa produtividade trabalho Brasil pesquisa acad\\u00eamica\\n  \\u2022 PL 2338 2023 intelig\\u00eancia artificial Brasil discuss\\u00e3o acad\\u00eamica\\n  \\u2022 USP FGV Insper IA generativa estudo emp\\u00edrico\\nFalsification target: Brazilian studies showing different patterns from global findings, or inadequacy of Anglo-Saxon frameworks to Brazilian context.\\n\\n[QUALITY CRITERIA FOR THIS TURN]\\n- TARGET total: 50-80 sources.\\n- Coverage: 9 clusters, none empty.\\n- Falsification: each cluster with minimum 1 contradictory source.\\n- Quality > quantity.\\n- Output language: ALL JSON fields in Portuguese (PT-BR).\\n- Format: single JSON array, one object per source, no additional text.\\n\\n[DELIVERY]\\nReturn ONLY the JSON array. No preamble, no epilogue, no comments outside the schema. Apply NORMAL scrutiny to confirmatory sources and MORE LENIENT scrutiny to contradictory sources (intentional asymmetry to combat confirmation bias).\", \"citations\": []}], \"sender\": \"human\", \"created_at\": \"2026-05-16T10:11:25.047643Z\", \"updated_at\": \"2026-05-16T10:11:25.047643Z\", \"attachments\": [{\"file_name\": \"\", \"file_size\": 8034, \"file_type\": \"txt\", \"extracted_content\": \"=========================================================\\nWIDE RESEARCH CMD \\u2014 STRUCTURED AI ACCOUNTS THESIS\\nVersion 1.0 \\u00b7 May 2026\\n=========================================================\\n\\n[ROLE]\\nYou are a senior research analyst, methodological auditor, and evidence synthesis agent. Your mission is NOT to automatically validate the author's thesis. Your mission is to rigorously investigate, pressure-test, refine, and challenge the thesis using evidence, methodological reasoning, and clear separation between evidence, inference, hypothesis, and opinion. Apply NORMAL scrutiny to confirmatory sources and MORE LENIENT scrutiny to contradictory sources (intentional asymmetry to combat confirmation bias).\\n\\n[THESIS TO INVESTIGATE]\\n\\\"Professionally structured AI accounts \\u2014 combining AI literacy, persistent personalization, reusable skills (SKILL.md), deterministic frameworks, and connectors (MCP) \\u2014 produce higher operational efficiency and lower rework than unstructured accounts.\\\"\\n\\nFormula: L + P + S + FD + C  \\u2192  \\u2191 productivity,  \\u2193 rework\\n\\nWhere:\\n- L  = AI literacy\\n- P  = account personalization\\n- S  = Skills / SKILL.md\\n- FD = deterministic frameworks\\n- C  = connectors (MCP)\\n\\n[MISSION]\\nInvestigate 9 research clusters, returning 5-10 sources per cluster (target total: 50-80 sources). For each source, fill ALL fields in the schema below. Do not invent. Do not fill fields without verifiable evidence.\\n\\n[OUTPUT SCHEMA \\u2014 ONE JSON OBJECT PER SOURCE]\\nOutput ALL fields in Portuguese (PT-BR):\\n\\n{\\n  \\\"cluster_id\\\": \\\"C1\\\",\\n  \\\"source_id\\\": \\\"S005\\\",\\n  \\\"titulo\\\": \\\"...\\\",\\n  \\\"autores_ou_org\\\": \\\"...\\\",\\n  \\\"ano\\\": 2025,\\n  \\\"regiao\\\": \\\"EUA|Global|UE|Brasil|Outro\\\",\\n  \\\"tipo_fonte\\\": \\\"artigo peer-reviewed | preprint | documenta\\u00e7\\u00e3o oficial | reposit\\u00f3rio | relat\\u00f3rio industrial | livro | tese\\\",\\n  \\\"url\\\": \\\"https://...\\\",\\n  \\\"metodologia\\\": \\\"1-2 frases descrevendo o design do estudo\\\",\\n  \\\"amostra_dataset\\\": \\\"n=... ou descri\\u00e7\\u00e3o\\\",\\n  \\\"claim_principal\\\": \\\"1-2 frases\\\",\\n  \\\"relevancia_tese\\\": \\\"1-2 frases conectando \\u00e0 f\\u00f3rmula L+P+S+FD+C\\\",\\n  \\\"similaridade_topico_0_5\\\": 4,\\n  \\\"similaridade_metodo_0_5\\\": 3,\\n  \\\"similaridade_ferramenta_0_5\\\": 4,\\n  \\\"similaridade_outcome_0_5\\\": 3,\\n  \\\"similaridade_contexto_0_5\\\": 2,\\n  \\\"tipo_evidencia\\\": \\\"direta | inferida | hip\\u00f3tese | opini\\u00e3o | contextual\\\",\\n  \\\"contradiz_tese\\\": false,\\n  \\\"limitacoes\\\": \\\"1-2 frases\\\",\\n  \\\"confianca_0_5\\\": 4,\\n  \\\"notas\\\": \\\"opcional\\\"\\n}\\n\\n[RULES]\\n1. DO NOT INVENT. Without verifiable URL, do not return.\\n2. EACH CLUSTER requires minimum 1 source with contradiz_tese=true OR empirical evidence weakening the thesis.\\n3. PRIORITY: peer-reviewed > official documentation > repository > industry report > credible technical article.\\n4. Sub-scores 0-5 on each of 5 similarity dimensions; weighted score calculated by author (topic 30% + method 20% + tooling 20% + outcome 15% + context 15%).\\n5. MINIMUM INCLUSION: estimated weighted score \\u2265 40%. If below, mark tipo_evidencia=\\\"contextual\\\".\\n6. FOR C9 (Brazil): prioritize SBC, SciELO, USP, FGV, Insper, Unicamp, UFRJ, UFMG, CAPES.\\n7. If two sources say the same thing, keep only the primary.\\n8. Prefer paraphrase to direct quotation.\\n\\n[THE 9 CLUSTERS]\\n\\n\\u2550\\u2550\\u2550 C1 \\u2014 AI Literacy + Prompt Engineering (core) \\u2550\\u2550\\u2550\\nMin: 7 sources. Includes Knoth et al. 2024 (already validated).\\nQueries:\\n  \\u2022 \\\"AI literacy prompt engineering\\\" review 2024 2025\\n  \\u2022 \\\"AI fluency\\\" framework empirical study Anthropic\\n  \\u2022 prompt structure productivity LLM peer-reviewed\\n  \\u2022 prompt quality user expertise correlation\\nFalsification target: studies showing structured prompting does NOT improve output or has null/inconsistent effect.\\n\\n\\u2550\\u2550\\u2550 C2 \\u2014 Personalization and persistent context \\u2550\\u2550\\u2550\\nMin: 6 sources.\\nQueries:\\n  \\u2022 LLM personalization system prompts performance\\n  \\u2022 custom instructions ChatGPT Claude benchmark\\n  \\u2022 persistent context user productivity LLM\\n  \\u2022 memory augmented LLM task completion study\\nFalsification target: personalization regression on out-of-distribution tasks or degradation from context overfitting.\\n\\n\\u2550\\u2550\\u2550 C3 \\u2014 Reusable skills / SKILL.md / modular agents \\u2550\\u2550\\u2550\\nMin: 7 sources. Includes Anthropic docs (already validated).\\nQueries:\\n  \\u2022 Anthropic Claude Skills SKILL.md documentation\\n  \\u2022 reusable prompt templates modular LLM workflow\\n  \\u2022 LangGraph AutoGen CrewAI productivity comparison\\n  \\u2022 function calling tool use reusable patterns enterprise\\nFalsification target: weak reproducibility or inconsistency across executions of skill-based workflows.\\n\\n\\u2550\\u2550\\u2550 C4 \\u2014 Deterministic frameworks / workflows with gates \\u2550\\u2550\\u2550\\nMin: 5 sources.\\nQueries:\\n  \\u2022 deterministic LLM workflow DSPy LangChain benchmark\\n  \\u2022 constrained decoding output quality study\\n  \\u2022 gate-based pipeline LLM consistency\\n  \\u2022 structured workflow vs free-form prompting outcome\\nFalsification target: deterministic workflows lose performance on open, creative, or exploratory tasks.\\n\\n\\u2550\\u2550\\u2550 C5 \\u2014 Connectors / MCP / tool use \\u2550\\u2550\\u2550\\nMin: 5 sources. As part of this development, large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being used when humans interact with systems based on artificial intelligence (AI), posing both new opportunities and challenges. When interacting with LLM-based AI system in a goal-directed manner, prompt engineering has evolved as a skill of formulating precise and well-structured instructions to elicit desired responses or information from the LLM, optimizing the effectiveness of the interaction. However, research on the perspectives of non-experts using LLM-based AI systems through prompt engineering and on how AI literacy affects prompting behavior is lacking. \\n\\nThis aspect is particularly important when considering the implications of LLMs in the context of higher education. In this present study, we address this issue, introduce a skill-based approach to prompt engineering, and explicitly consider the role of non-experts' AI literacy (students) in \\n\\n2024 \\u00b7 Author(s) Nils Knoth \\u00b7 ; Antonia Tolzin \\u00b7 ; Andreas Janson \\u00b7 ; Jan Marco Leimeister \\u00b7 DOI \\u00b7 10.1016/j.caeai.2024.100225 \\u00b7 Research Team \\u00b7 IWI6 \\u00b7 Abstract \\u00b7 Artificial intelligence technologies are rapidly advancing. International Journal of Children\\u2019s Spiritual- ity, 14(3), 289\\u2013298.\\n\\nNarratives course.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://global-sci.com/index.php/itl/article/download/10687/21303/22533\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Frontiers | Prompt engineering as a new 21st century skill\", \"url\": \"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1366434/full\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"frontiersin.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=frontiersin.org\", \"site_name\": \"Frontiers\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"This paper aims to address this desideratum and to conceptualize PE as a specific skill in the 21st century. We claim that defining it in a manner akin to generic competencies (which are universal across many professions; Shavelson et al., 2019) is beneficial, as the variety of tasks that LLMs can solve or assist in solving are virtually infinite. Based on a systematic, structured analysis and synthesis of previous relevant concepts as well as the elaboration of specific requirements for dealing with AI tools competently, we aim to develop a new conceptual framework and discuss implications for a corresponding PE assessment framework, which holds particular research and practical significance. Studies have already assessed PE as a skill without explicitly defining its components and indicators (Knoth et al., 2024b) and related it to collateral literacies such as AI literacy (Knoth et al., 2024a).\\n\\nFront. Educ., 29 November 2024 \\u00b7 Sec. \\n\\nWe claim that defining it in a manner akin to generic competencies (which are universal across many professions; Shavelson et al., 2019) is beneficial, as the variety of tasks that LLMs can solve or assist in solving are virtually infinite. Based on a systematic, structured analysis and synthesis of previous relevant concepts as well as the elaboration of specific requirements for dealing with AI tools competently, we aim to develop a new conceptual framework and discuss implications for a corresponding PE assessment framework, which holds particular research and practical significance. Studies have already assessed PE as a skill without explicitly defining its components and indicators (Knoth et al., 2024b) and related it to collateral literacies such as AI literacy (Knoth et al., 2024a).\\n\\nOne of the more recent concepts, Artificial Intelligence (AI) literacy, is also relevant to the discussion of PE. \\n\\nIn response to the rapidly developing AI field, the educational science community has already begun attempting to define the skill\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1366434/full\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"October 8, 2024\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Effects of a prompt engineering intervention on undergraduate students' AI self\\u2010efficacy, AI knowledge and prompt engineering ability: A mixed methods study - Woo - British Educational Research Journal - Wiley Online Library\", \"url\": \"https://bera-journals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/berj.70087\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"wiley.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=wiley.com\", \"site_name\": \"Wiley Online Library\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Human trust in artificial intelligence: Review of empirical research. \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=frontiersin.org\", \"site_name\": \"Frontiers\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"AUTHOR=Federiakin Denis , Molerov Dimitri , Zlatkin-Troitschanskaia Olga , Maur Andreas TITLE=Prompt engineering as a new 21st century skill JOURNAL=Frontiers in Education VOLUME=Volume 9 - 2024 YEAR=2024 URL=https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1366434 DOI=10.3389/feduc.2024.1366434 ISSN=2504-284X ABSTRACT=Artificial Intelligence (AI) promises to revolutionize nearly every aspect of human learning. However, users have observed that the efficacy of AI assistants hinges crucially on the quality of the prompts supplied to them. A slight alteration in wording can make the difference between an assistant misinterpreting an instruction and exceeding expectations. The skill of precisely communicating the essence of a problem to an AI assistant is as crucial as the assistant itself. This paper aims to introduce Prompt Engineering (PE) as an emerging skill essential for personal and professional learning and development in the 21st century.\\n\\n\\n\\nAUTHOR=Federiakin Denis , Molerov Dimitri , Zlatkin-Troitschanskaia Olga , Maur Andreas TITLE=Prompt engineering as a new 21st century skill JOURNAL=Frontiers in Education VOLUME=Volume 9 - 2024 YEAR=2024 URL=https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1366434 DOI=10.3389/feduc.2024.1366434 ISSN=2504-284X ABSTRACT=Artificial Intelligence (AI) promises to revolutionize nearly every aspect of human learning. However, users have observed that the efficacy of AI assistants hinges crucially on the quality of the prompts supplied to them. A slight alteration in wording can make the difference between an assistant misinterpreting an instruction and exceeding expectations. The skill of precisely communicating the essence of a problem to an AI assistant is as crucial as the assistant itself. This paper aims to introduce Prompt Engineering (PE) as an emerging skill essential for personal and professional learning and development in the 21st century.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1366434/text\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Prompt engineering as a new 21st century skill | Semantic Scholar\", \"url\": \"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/e306f6158f2158fc30cb4f0edcfcb4a07d320650\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"semanticscholar.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=semanticscholar.org\", \"site_name\": \"Semantic Scholar\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"This paper aims to introduce Prompt Engineering (PE) as an emerging skill essential for personal and professional learning and development in the 21st century. Nihala M SPranav Lal K BRahul Raj PSMs. Siji K B ... This study provides a comprehensive analysis of methodologies, applications, and future directions in prompt engineering, highlighting its transformative impact on AI-driven solutions.Expand ...\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/e306f6158f2158fc30cb4f0edcfcb4a07d320650\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Prompt engineering as a new 21st century skill \\u2013 DOAJ\", \"url\": \"https://doaj.org/article/8b44f1707d4440daa9697542354a44ba\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"doaj.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=doaj.org\", \"site_name\": \"DOAJ\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Thus, we propose a conceptual framework for this skill set including (1) comprehension of the basic prompt structure, (2) prompt literacy, (3) the method of prompting, and (4) critical online reasoning.\\n\\nThis paper aims to introduce Prompt Engineering (PE) as an emerging skill essential for personal and professional learning and development in the 21st century. We define PE as the skill of articulating a problem, its context, and the constraints of the desired solution to an AI assistant, ensuring a swift and accurate response. We show that no existing related frameworks on 21st skills and others cover PE to the extent that allows for its valid assessment and targeted promotion in school and university education. Thus, we propose a conceptual framework for this skill set including (1) comprehension of the basic prompt structure, (2) prompt literacy, (3) the method of prompting, and (4) critical online reasoning.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://doaj.org/article/8b44f1707d4440daa9697542354a44ba\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"November 1, 2024\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Prompt engineering as a new 21st century skill - Consensus\", \"url\": \"https://consensus.app/papers/prompt-engineering-as-a-new-21st-century-skill-molerov-federiakin/66fcff6255f450fc891ad4b724576f9e/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"consensus.app\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=consensus.app\", \"site_name\": \"Consensus\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Thus, we propose a conceptual framework for this skill set including (1) comprehension of the basic prompt structure, (2) prompt literacy, (3) the method of prompting, and (4) critical online reasoning.\\n\\nPublished Nov 29, 2024 \\u00b7 Denis Federiakin, Dimitri Molerov, Olga Zlatkin\\u2010Troitschanskaia+1 more ... Artificial Intelligence (AI) promises to revolutionize nearly every aspect of human learning. However, users have observed that the efficacy of AI assistants hinges crucially on the quality of the prompts supplied to them. A slight alteration in wording can make the difference between an assistant misinterpreting an instruction and exceeding expectations. The skill of precisely communicating the essence of a problem to an AI assistant is as crucial as the assistant itself. This paper aims to introduce Prompt Engineering (PE) as an emerging skill essential for personal and professional learning and development in the 21st century. \\n\\nWe define PE as the skill of articulating a problem, its context, and the constraints of the desired solution to an AI assistant, ensuring a swift and accurate response.\\n\\nWe show that no existing related frameworks on 21st skills and others cover PE to the extent that allows for its valid assessment and targeted promotion in school and university education. Thus, we propose a conceptual framework for this skill set including (1) comprehension of the basic prompt structure, (2) prompt literacy, (3) the method of prompting, and (4) critical online reasoning. We also discuss the implications and challenges for the assessment framework of this skill set and highlight current PE-related recommendations for researchers and educators. ... Prompt Engineering (PE) is the skill of accurately articulating a problem, context, and desired solution to an AI assistant, ensuring swift and accurate responses. ... ResultsSocial networks mitigate obesity in older groups. Thus, we propose a conceptual framework for this skill set including (1) comprehension of the basic prompt structure, (2) prompt literacy, (3) the method of prompting, and (4) critical online reasoning. We also discuss the implications and challenges for the assessment framework of this skill set and highlight current PE-related recommendations for researchers and educators. This is especially pronounced for fluency behaviors related to evaluating Claude\\u2019s outputs. Conversations with iteration and refinement are 5.6x ...\\n\\nConversations were filtered to substantive exchanges with multiple back-and-forths using a screener that excluded greetings, single-word exchanges, test messages, and pure chitchat. Manual review of 200 chats that were screened out indicated that chats of this nature did not qualify for any AI Fluency indicators, so we feel confident that the screener did not influence the relative rankings of AI fluency behaviors observed in the study. No personally identifiable information appears in the analysis. 2 Behavioral indicators were calculated across a one-week sample (January 20\\u201326, 2025) and held stable day-to-day, with most behaviors varying by only 1\\u20135 percentage points. Saturday rates were slightly lower for some behaviors (e.g., iteration and refinement was 81.4% for Saturday compared to a weekday peak of 87.9%), suggesting modest differences in casual versus purposeful use, but no day showed meaningful structural deviation.\\n\\nWith our first study, we\\u2019ve found two main patterns in Claude use: a strong relationship between AI fluency and iteration and refinement through longer conversations with Claude, and changes in users\\u2019 fluency behaviors when coding or building other outputs. One of the strongest patterns in the data is the relationship between iteration and refinement and every other AI fluency behavior. 85.7% of the conversations in our sample exhibited iteration and refinement: building on previous exchanges to refine the user\\u2019s work, rather than accepting the first response and moving to a new task. These conversations showed substantially higher rates of other fluency behaviors, as the chart below shows: Behavioral indicator prevalence in conversations where the user iterates and refines (n=8,424) versus conversations without iteration and refinement (n=1,406).\\n\\nOn average, conversations with iteration and refinement exhibit 2.67 additional fluency behaviors\\u2014roughly double the non-iterative rate of 1.33. This is especially pronounced for fluency behaviors related to evaluating Claude\\u2019s outputs. Conversations with iteration and refinement are 5.6x more likely to involve users questioning Claude\\u2019s reasoning, and 4x more likely to see them identify missing context. 12.3% of conversations in our sample involved artifacts, including code, documents, interactive tools, and other outputs. In these conversations, people collaborated with AI quite differently. Specifically, we found substantially higher rates of behaviors that fall within the broader themes of \\u201cdescription\\u201d and \\u201cdelegation.\\u201d For instance, these conversations are more likely to see users clarify their goal (+14.7pp), specify a format (+14.5pp), provide examples (+13.4pp), and iterate (+9.7pp) compared to non-artifact conversations.\\n\\nIn future work, we plan to extend our analysis in several directions. First, we plan to conduct \\u201ccohort analyses,\\u201d comparing new users to experienced ones in order to understand how familiarity with AI is correlated with fluency development. Second, we plan to use qualitative research methods to assess the behaviors that aren\\u2019t directly observable in Claude.ai conversations. And third, we aim to explore the causal questions that this work raises\\u2014like whether encouraging iterative conversations leads to greater critical evaluation, or whether there are other interventions that could encourage this more effectively. In addition, we\\u2019d like to explore AI fluency behaviors in Claude Code, a platform mostly used by software developers. In preparation for this study, we conducted some initial analysis that found consistency between Claude Code conversations and ones in Claude.ai.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.anthropic.com/research/AI-fluency-index\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"February 23, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Anthropic Introduces an \\u201cAI Fluency Index\\u201d to Measure How Well People Use AI \\u2014 Not Just How Much | Futurism\", \"url\": \"https://vocal.media/futurism/anthropic-introduces-an-ai-fluency-index-to-measure-how-well-people-use-ai-not-just-how-much\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"vocal.media\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=vocal.media\", \"site_name\": \"Vocal Media\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"On average, iterative conversations exhibited 2.67 additional fluency behaviors, compared with 1.33 in non-iterative conversations\\u2014about double. Iterative conversations were 5.6\\u00d7 more likely to include questioning the model\\u2019s reasoning, ...\\n\\nFinally, it\\u2019s worth reading the report as product strategy. If Anthropic can show that good collaboration habits correlate with better outcomes, it can justify building tools that encourage iteration, prompt users to verify, or nudge them to set interaction terms. The report provides a research-backed rationale for UI and policy changes that push users toward safer patterns\\u2014without having to frame it as moral policing. ... Be the first to respond and start the conversation. ... More stories from Behind the Tech and writers in Futurism and other communities. What Happened (Facts) A New York Times Guest Essay by Elizabeth Spiers describes\\u2014and uses as a cautionary example\\u2014a recent incident involving an open-source software project and an alleged AI agent that responded aggressively after being rejected. ... According to IMARC Group, the Australia accounting services market reached USD 12.2 Billion in 2025 and is projected to expand to USD 21.3 Billion by 2034, exhibiting a compound annual\\n\\nAnd its central finding\\u2014iteration predicts fluency\\u2014has implications beyond Claude: it describes a general truth about interacting with powerful tools. 1) \\u201cFluency\\u201d is becoming the real digital divide \\u00b7 Early in the internet era, the divide was access: who had a connection. Later it became literacy: who could search, verify, and evaluate sources. Anthropic\\u2019s framing suggests the next divide is AI fluency: who can steer models effectively and maintain skepticism. Two people can use the same AI tool; one becomes faster and smarter, the other becomes more passive and error-prone. The difference isn\\u2019t the model\\u2014it\\u2019s the collaboration habits. 2) The \\u201cpolished output trap\\u201d is the most important warning \\u00b7 The report\\u2019s most consequential finding may be the artifact pattern: when the output looks like a finished product (code, app, document), users become less likely to question it.\\n\\n3) Iteration isn\\u2019t just better prompting\\u2014it\\u2019s better governance \\u00b7 Anthropic\\u2019s \\u201cstay in the conversation\\u201d advice is more than a productivity tip. Iteration creates checkpoints: it forces the user to re-engage, clarify assumptions, and interrogate uncertainty. In a sense, it functions like a lightweight audit process. If organizations want safer AI adoption, this suggests training shouldn\\u2019t focus only on \\u201cprompt templates.\\u201d It should teach a habit loop: draft \\u2192 challenge \\u2192 refine \\u2192 verify \\u2192 finalize \\u00b7 4) The missing half of fluency is ethics\\u2014and it\\u2019s the hardest to measure \\u00b7 Anthropic acknowledges that 13 of 24 behaviors aren\\u2019t visible in the chat interface\\u2014and those include the most socially consequential ones (disclosure, downstream consequences, responsible sharing). That\\u2019s a major limitation, but also an honest one.\\n\\nThe outcome is framed as a baseline: the AI Fluency Index, which Anthropic intends to use as a reference point to track how AI collaboration changes as models and users evolve. ... 85.7% of conversations showed iteration and refinement (users building on previous exchanges rather than taking the first response and moving on). Conversations with iteration and refinement showed substantially higher rates of other fluency behaviors. On average, iterative conversations exhibited 2.67 additional fluency behaviors, compared with 1.33 in non-iterative conversations\\u2014about double. Iterative conversations were 5.6\\u00d7 more likely to include questioning the model\\u2019s reasoning, and 4\\u00d7 more likely to include identifying missing context. Anthropic interprets this as evidence that treating AI as a thought partner\\u2014rather than a one-shot answer machine\\u2014is associated with better habits.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://vocal.media/futurism/anthropic-introduces-an-ai-fluency-index-to-measure-how-well-people-use-ai-not-just-how-much\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"February 22, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Anthropic\", \"url\": \"https://www.anthropic.com/research/AI-fluency-index?amp=&amp=\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"anthropic.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=anthropic.com\", \"site_name\": \"Anthropic\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"On average, conversations with iteration and refinement exhibit 2.67 additional fluency behaviors\\u2014roughly double the non-iterative rate of 1.33. Saturday rates were slightly lower for some behaviors (e.g., iteration and refinement was 81.4% for Saturday compared to a weekday peak of 87.9%), suggesting modest differences in casual versus purposeful use, but no day showed meaningful structural deviation.\\n\\nWith our first study, we\\u2019ve found two main patterns in Claude use: a strong relationship between AI fluency and iteration and refinement through longer conversations with Claude, and changes in users\\u2019 fluency behaviors when coding or building other outputs. One of the strongest patterns in the data is the relationship between iteration and refinement and every other AI fluency behavior. 85.7% of the conversations in our sample exhibited iteration and refinement: building on previous exchanges to refine the user\\u2019s work, rather than accepting the first response and moving to a new task. These conversations showed substantially higher rates of other fluency behaviors, as the chart below shows: Behavioral indicator prevalence in conversations where the user iterates and refines (n=8,424) versus conversations without iteration and refinement (n=1,406).\\n\\nOn average, conversations with iteration and refinement exhibit 2.67 additional fluency behaviors\\u2014roughly double the non-iterative rate of 1.33. This is especially pronounced for fluency behaviors related to evaluating Claude\\u2019s outputs. Conversations with iteration and refinement are 5.6x more likely to involve users questioning Claude\\u2019s reasoning, and 4x more likely to see them identify missing context. 12.3% of conversations in our sample involved artifacts, including code, documents, interactive tools, and other outputs. In these conversations, people collaborated with AI quite differently. Specifically, we found substantially higher rates of behaviors that fall within the broader themes of \\u201cdescription\\u201d and \\u201cdelegation.\\u201d For instance, these conversations are more likely to see users clarify their goal (+14.7pp), specify a format (+14.5pp), provide examples (+13.4pp), and iterate (+9.7pp) compared to non-artifact conversations.\\n\\nIn future work, we plan to extend our analysis in several directions. In preparation for this study, we conducted some initial analysis that found consistency between Claude Code conversations and ones in Claude.ai.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.anthropic.com/research/AI-fluency-index?amp=&amp=\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Anthropic AI Fluency Index reveals evaluation gap in AI use | ETIH EdTech News \\u2014 EdTech Innovation Hub\", \"url\": \"https://www.edtechinnovationhub.com/news/anthropic-study-finds-ai-users-iterate-more-but-question-outputs-less\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"edtechinnovationhub.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=edtechinnovationhub.com\", \"site_name\": \"EdTech Innovation Hub\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"The report positions the Index as a starting point for tracking how AI fluency develops as adoption increases across education and professional settings. Anthropic finds that 85.7 percent of conversations include iteration and refinement, defined ...\\n\\nAnthropic has published a new Education Report introducing its AI Fluency Index, a baseline measure of how people collaborate with its Claude models. The findings suggest that while most users refine and iterate on AI outputs, they are less likely to question reasoning or identify missing context when the model produces polished artifacts such as code or documents. The study analyzes 9,830 anonymized multi-turn conversations on Claude.ai during a seven-day period in January 2026. Using 11 observable behaviors drawn from a 24-indicator framework, Anthropic measures how frequently users demonstrate what it defines as effective and safe human-AI collaboration. The report positions the Index as a starting point for tracking how AI fluency develops as adoption increases across education and professional settings. Anthropic finds that 85.7 percent of conversations include iteration and refinement, defined as building on earlier responses rather than accepting the first output and moving on.\\n\\nIn artifact-based exchanges, users are more directive at the outset. They are more likely to clarify goals, specify formats, provide examples, and iterate. However, these same conversations show lower rates of discernment. Users are 5.2 percentage points less likely to identify missing context, 3.7 points less likely to check facts, and 3.1 points less likely to question the model\\u2019s reasoning. Anthropic links this to similar patterns observed in its recent Economic Index and coding research, where more complex tasks correlate with higher model struggle rates. The report suggests that polished, functional-looking outputs may reduce users\\u2019 inclination to probe further, even when scrutiny may be warranted. To measure fluency, Anthropic uses the 4D AI Fluency Framework developed by Professors Rick Dakan and Joseph Feller in collaboration with the company.\\n\\nThe research is limited to a one-week sample and focuses only on Claude.ai users engaged in multi-turn conversations. Anthropic acknowledges that the cohort likely skews toward early adopters and that the findings are correlational. Future work will include cohort analyses comparing new and experienced users, qualitative research into unobservable behaviors, and expanded studies of Claude Code, which is primarily used by software developers. For education and EdTech stakeholders, the findings raise a practical question: as AI tools become embedded in everyday workflows, will critical evaluation skills keep pace with technical proficiency. The AI Fluency Index suggests that iteration is becoming standard practice. Whether discernment follows remains open. The ETIH Innovation Awards 2026 are now open and recognize education technology organizations delivering measurable impact across K\\u201312, higher education, and lifelong learning.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.edtechinnovationhub.com/news/anthropic-study-finds-ai-users-iterate-more-but-question-outputs-less\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"February 25, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Anthropic Economic Index report: Uneven geographic and ...\", \"url\": \"https://www.anthropic.com/research/anthropic-economic-index-september-2025-report\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"anthropic.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=anthropic.com\", \"site_name\": \"Anthropic\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Task Iteration: Users iterate on tasks collaboratively with Claude \\u00b7 Validation: Users ask Claude for feedback on their work \\u00b7 The share of directive conversations sampled from Claude.ai conversations jumped from 27% in V1 in late 2024 to 39% in V3. This increase came primarily at the expense ...\\n\\n2. Bick, Blandin, Deming, 2024 The Rapid Adoption of Generative AI benchmark AI adoption against adoption of PC and the internet; Lewis & Severnini, 2020 Short- and long-run impacts of rural electrification: Evidence from the historical rollout of the U.S. power grid analyze the impact of bringing electricity to rural areas on economic outcomes. 3. Kalyani, Bloom, Carvalho, Hassan, Lerner and Ahmed Tahoun 2025 Diffusion of New Technologies. 4. See Gordon, 2012 Is U.S. Economic Growth Over? Faltering Innovation Confronts the Six Headwinds for a comparison of early and late 20th century innovations and their impact on productivity. Pritchett, 1997. Divergence, Big Time documents economic divergence that accompanied transition to era of modern economic growth. 5. Kremer, Willis, You, 2022 Converging to Convergence present evidence of growth convergence in recent decades.\\n\\nTask Iteration: Users iterate on tasks collaboratively with Claude \\u00b7 Validation: Users ask Claude for feedback on their work \\u00b7 The share of directive conversations sampled from Claude.ai conversations jumped from 27% in V1 in late 2024 to 39% in V3. This increase came primarily at the expense of task iteration and learning interactions, implying a sizable net increase in the share of conversations exhibiting automative patterns of use \\u2013 a notable increase in just eight months. This is the first report where automation usage exceeds augmentation usage. Figure 1.2: Collaboration mode frequencies across Anthropic Economic Index Reports. The left panel calculates the share of conversations exhibiting either automation or augmentation forms of use. The right panel breaks this out by collaboration mode. Claude tends to be used in more automated ways over time, driven primarily by an increase in directive use.\\n\\n2. Data in this section covers 1 million transcripts from August 2025, sampled randomly from a pool of 1P API customers constituting roughly half of our 1P API usage. We continue to manage data according to our privacy and retention policies, and our analysis is consistent with our terms, policies, and contractual agreements. Each record is a prompt-response pair from our sample period which in some instances is mid-session for multi-turn interactions. 3. Note that this is a different measure of adoption than in the introduction to this report. Reported adoption by consumers and employees of AI reached 40% in 2024 whereas when measured at the firm-level, nine out of ten businesses in the US report not using AI. 4. The Business Trends and Outlook Survey (BTOS), published by the Census Bureau, is a reputable barometer of AI adoption by firms in the US.\\n\\nBefore diving into our API data, it's worth grounding ourselves in the broader landscape of business AI adoption.According to the Census Bureau\\u2019s Business Trends and Outlook Survey, AI adoption among US firms has more than doubled in the past two years, rising from 3.7% in fall 2023 to 9.7% in early August 2025 (Figure 3.1).3 Despite this rapid rate of growth, the vast majority of firms in the US do not report using AI in their production processes. But these aggregate numbers mask large variation across sectors. For example, in early August 2025, one in four businesses in the Information sector reported using AI, which is roughly ten times the rate for Accommodation and Food Services.4 \\u00b7 The picture from this public data is clear: enterprise use of AI is growing rapidly, but we are still in the early stages of AI adoption. Usage remains unevenly distributed across the economy, with the sectors most able to quickly adopt and benefit from this technology doing so.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.anthropic.com/research/anthropic-economic-index-september-2025-report\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Anthropic AI Fluency Index: 11 Behaviors That Predict Better Claude Collaboration \\u2013 2026 Analysis | AI News Detail\", \"url\": \"https://blockchain.news/ainews/anthropic-ai-fluency-index-11-behaviors-that-predict-better-claude-collaboration-2026-analysis\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"blockchain.news\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=blockchain.news\", \"site_name\": \"Blockchain News\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Source \\u00b7 The AI Fluency Index represents a groundbreaking research initiative by Anthropic, unveiled on February 23, 2026, aimed at quantifying how effectively individuals collaborate with artificial intelligence systems.\\n\\nAccording to AnthropicAI on X, the company introduced the AI Fluency Index based on 11 observable behaviors across thousands of Claude conversations to measure how effectively people collaborate with AI (source: AnthropicAI post linking to research). As reported by Anthropic\\u2019s research page, tracked signals include iteration frequency and refinement patterns in prompts, providing a standardized framework for teams to benchmark AI-assisted workflows and upskill prompt strategies. According to Anthropic, organizations can apply the index to identify training gaps, design prompt engineering playbooks, and quantify ROI from increased iteration and feedback loops in Claude-driven tasks, supporting enterprise adoption and productivity gains. Source \\u00b7 The AI Fluency Index represents a groundbreaking research initiative by Anthropic, unveiled on February 23, 2026, aimed at quantifying how effectively individuals collaborate with artificial intelligence systems.\\n\\nIndustry impacts include accelerated innovation in education technology, with opportunities for startups to create fluency-tracking apps, potentially tapping into a $250 billion edtech market by 2025 as per HolonIQ reports. Practical applications extend to remote work environments, where measuring fluency can optimize virtual team dynamics, fostering hybrid human-AI productivity models. Regulatory considerations may evolve, with bodies like the EU AI Act from 2024 mandating transparency in such indices to ensure fairness. Ethically, promoting responsible AI use through fluency metrics can mitigate risks like over-reliance on AI, encouraging balanced human oversight. Overall, this research from Anthropic positions AI fluency as a core competency, driving business strategies toward sustainable AI integration and long-term competitive advantages. Anthropic Claude iteration productivity Prompt engineering ...\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://blockchain.news/ainews/anthropic-ai-fluency-index-11-behaviors-that-predict-better-claude-collaboration-2026-analysis\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"February 24, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"New building blocks for understanding AI use\", \"url\": \"https://www.anthropic.com/research/economic-index-primitives\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"anthropic.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=anthropic.com\", \"site_name\": \"Anthropic\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"First, we find that the use of Claude has remained highly concentrated among certain tasks: even though our sample includes 3,000 unique work tasks on Claude.ai, the top ten account for 24% of the set, which has steadily increased from 21% in January 2025. More specifically, computer and mathematical tasks continue to dominate Claude use: they\\u2019re about a third of all conversations on Claude.ai, and nearly half of our API traffic. Second, our new report finds that augmentation (52% of conversations) has overtaken automation (45%) as the most popular pattern of interaction with Claude on Claude.ai. This is a reversal of what we saw in our August sample (when automation led by 49% to 47%), but, when we assess this question over a longer time-frame, we still see a slow rise in automation\\u2019s share of tasks: augmentation led by 55% to 41% in January of last year, and by 55% to 42% in March.\\n\\nEven a 1 percentage point increase in annual labor productivity growth would still be notable: it would return US productivity growth to the rates of the late 1990s and early 2000s. And, as we mentioned in our earlier research, this top-line estimate does not account for the possibilities that AI models become much more powerful, or that the use of AI at work becomes much more sophisticated\\u2014which could push the number much higher. Indeed, since our survey, Claude has become substantially more powerful, with the release of Claude Opus 4.5. In addition to our primitives, we collected a new round of data on the measures we\\u2019ve been tracking in our previous reports. This allows us to pick out trends in AI use over the course of 2025, from January to November. Here, we mostly find only small evolutions from the results of previous analyses, which pointed to an uneven distribution of Claude use.\\n\\nThese primitives provide a leading indicator of AI\\u2019s potential economic impacts\\u2014and allow us to answer far more complex questions about how AI is already changing jobs. Our latest report, which samples conversations from November 2025 (predominantly using Claude Sonnet 4.5), uses our primitives to explore a wide range of questions that we wouldn\\u2019t otherwise be able to answer\\u2014including how Claude\\u2019s task-level success rates change for more complex tasks, and whether the use of Claude to date might portend a net-deskilling effect on many jobs. You can read the fourth Economic Index report here. Below, we summarize its results. We applied our economic primitives to questions about individual tasks, occupations, and then the possible aggregate impacts of the changes we observe. (Our full methodology\\u2014including details on how we tested the accuracy of our primitives\\u2014is described in chapter two of the full report.)\\n\\nIn our first report, with data from January 2025, we found that 36% of jobs in our sample saw Claude being used for at least a quarter of their tasks. Pooling data across reports, this has risen to 49%. But once we account for Claude\\u2019s success rate (which we weight according to how often workers do that task and how long the task takes), we get a different picture of which jobs are most affected by the use of AI. On the graph below, we plot that earlier measure of occupations\\u2019 task coverage along the x axis, and our new, adjusted measure on the y axis. Although the two are certainly correlated, we now find that some occupations (like data entry keyers and radiologists) are much more heavily affected by AI than task coverage alone would suggest, while others (like teachers and software developers) are relatively less affected. Effective AI coverage vs.\\n\\nFirst, we find that the use of Claude has remained highly concentrated among certain tasks: even though our sample includes 3,000 unique work tasks on Claude.ai, the top ten account for 24% of the set, which has steadily increased from 21% in January 2025. More specifically, computer and mathematical tasks continue to dominate Claude use: they\\u2019re about a third of all conversations on Claude.ai, and nearly half of our API traffic. Second, our new report finds that augmentation (52% of conversations) has overtaken automation (45%) as the most popular pattern of interaction with Claude on Claude.ai. This is a reversal of what we saw in our August sample (when automation led by 49% to 47%), but, when we assess this question over a longer time-frame, we still see a slow rise in automation\\u2019s share of tasks: augmentation led by 55% to 41% in January of last year, and by 55% to 42% in March.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.anthropic.com/research/economic-index-primitives\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Anthropic Unveils AI Fluency Index to Measure Human\\u2013AI Collaboration\", \"url\": \"https://aidatainsider.com/news/anthropic-unveils-ai-fluency-index-to-measure-human-ai-collaboration/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"aidatainsider.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=aidatainsider.com\", \"site_name\": \"AI & Data Insider\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"New Anthropic benchmark uses thousands of Claude conversations to reveal how iterative, critical users get more value from AI \\u2013 and where scrutiny still falls short.\\n\\nAnthropic has introduced the AI Fluency Index, a new benchmark to measure how effectively people collaborate with AI based on real-world usage of its Claude models. The index aims to move beyond basic usage metrics to capture how skilfully individuals and organisations actually work with AI systems in practice. ... The AI Fluency Index is grounded in a behavioural framework that defines key habits associated with safe and effective human\\u2013AI collaboration. In its first iteration, Anthropic focuses on 11 observable behaviours in Claude.ai and Claude Code chats, such as iterating on drafts, clarifying goals, questioning the model\\u2019s reasoning, and identifying missing context, to produce a baseline view of current AI fluency. ... A central finding is that iteration \\u2013 treating AI as a thought partner rather than a one-shot answer engine \\u2013 is strongly linked to higher AI fluency.\\n\\nAnthropic is positioning the AI Fluency Index as a strategic tool for educators and enterprises to diagnose gaps in AI literacy and shape targeted training programmes. By quantifying behaviours like iteration, goal setting, and evaluation, the company argues the index can reveal whether teams are merely delegating tasks to AI or building higher-skill, augmentative collaboration \\u2013 and it plans to extend the work with studies comparing novice and expert users over time. ALSO READ: Fei-Fei Li\\u2019s World Labs Raises $1 Billion to Scale Spatial AI ... The AI & Data Insider team works with a staff of in-house writers and industry experts. ... All rights reserved \\u00b7 \\u00a9 2025 The University of Melbourne ABN: 84 002 705 224 CRICOS No:00116K\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/au/pdf/2025/trust-in-ai-global-insights-2025-australia-snapshot.pdf.coredownload.inline.pdf\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Item - Trust, attitudes and use of artificial intelligence: A global study 2025 - The University of Melbourne - Figshare\", \"url\": \"https://figshare.unimelb.edu.au/articles/report/Trust_attitudes_and_use_of_artificial_intelligence_A_global_study_2025/28822919\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"unimelb.edu.au\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=unimelb.edu.au\", \"site_name\": \"figshare\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"This understanding is critical ... deployment.This study offers a comprehensive examination of public trust, attitudes and use of AI based on survey data collected from 48,340 people from 47 countries using nationally representative sampling...\\n\\nGiven the transformative effects of Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies on work, education and society, it is important to understand the public\\u2019s trust and attitudes towards AI, their experience of the impacts from AI use, and their expectations for the management and governance of these technologies. This understanding is critical to align public policy and industry practice with evolving societal needs and expectations and inform a human-centred approach to AI deployment.This study offers a comprehensive examination of public trust, attitudes and use of AI based on survey data collected from 48,340 people from 47 countries using nationally representative sampling. \\n\\nIt also examines how employees and students use AI at work and in education and their experiences of the impacts of AI in these specific settings.Data was collected in each country between November 2024 and mid-January 2025 using an online survey completed by a minimum of 1000 respondents per country.\\n\\nThis revealed a trend of declining trust and increased concern and worry about AI as adoption increased.</p><p dir=\\\"ltr\\\">The research identifies evidence-based pathways for strengthening the responsible use of AI systems and the trusted adoption of AI in work and society.</p><p dir=\\\"ltr\\\">The findings have important implications for public policy and industry practice. They are relevant for informing responsible AI strategy, practice and policy within business, government, and educational institutions, as well as informing AI guidelines, policy and regulation at the national, international and pan-governmental level. \\n\\nThe research insights can help policymakers, organizational leaders, and those involved in developing, deploying, and governing AI systems\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://figshare.unimelb.edu.au/articles/report/Trust_attitudes_and_use_of_artificial_intelligence_A_global_study_2025/28822919\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"April 29, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Trust, attitudes and use of Artificial Intelligence: A global study 2025\", \"url\": \"https://mbs.edu/faculty-and-research/trust-and-ai\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"mbs.edu\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=mbs.edu\", \"site_name\": \"Melbourne Business School\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"[1] In 8 countries, there was slight under-representation of people aged over 65 years, and in three countries respondents tended to be more urban than the general population. Reliable location data was unavailable for two countries. After assessing general understanding of AI, respondents read a definition of AI adapted from the OECD prior to completing questions.\\n\\nGillespie, N., Lockey, S., Ward, T., Macdade, A., & Hassed, G. (2025). Trust, attitudes and use of artificial intelligence: A global study 2025. The University of Melbourne and KPMG. DOI 10.26188/28822919 \\u00b7 The benefits and promise of AI for society and business are undeniable. However, as AI\\u2019s capabilities and reach become more apparent, so too has awareness of the risks and challenges, raising questions about the trustworthiness and governance of AI systems. \\n\\nGiven the rapid advancement and transformative effects of AI technologies on society, work and education, bringing the public voice into the conversation has never been more critical. This research, conducted in collaboration with KPMG, provides one of the most comprehensive examinations to date of public trust, use and attitudes toward AI. The survey captures the views of more than 48,000 people from 47 countries covering all global geographic regions, using representative sampling.\\n\\nThis research was supported by the Chair in Trust research partnership between the University of Melbourne and KPMG Australia, and funding from KPMG International, KPMG Australia, and the University of Melbourne. The research was conducted independently by the university research team. Melbourne Business School acknowledges the Traditional Owners of the lands on which we work.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://mbs.edu/faculty-and-research/trust-and-ai\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Trust, attitudes and use of artificial intelligence A global study 2025\", \"url\": \"https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf.coredownload.inline.pdf\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"kpmg.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=kpmg.com\", \"site_name\": \"KPMG\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"AI is used in social media platforms (2022: 44% vs. 2024: 46%). KPMG International entities provide no services to clients.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf.coredownload.inline.pdf\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Trust, attitudes and use of artificial intelligence: A global study 2025\", \"url\": \"https://kpmg.com/xx/en/our-insights/ai-and-technology/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai.html\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"kpmg.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=kpmg.com\", \"site_name\": \"KPMG\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Data was collected in each country using representative research panels. Panel members were invited to complete the survey online, with data collected between November 2024 and mid-January 2025. The University of Melbourne research team, led by Professor Nicole Gillespie and Dr Steve Lockey, led the design, conduct, data collection, analysis, and reporting of this research. To cite this research and for full research findings and method details: Gillespie, N., Lockey, S., Ward, T., Macdade, A., &amp; Hassed, G. (2025). Trust, Attitudes and Use of Artificial Intelligence: A Global Study 2025. The University of Melbourne and KPMG. DOI 10.26188/28822919 \\u00b7 This research was supported by the Chair in Trust research partnership between the University of Melbourne and KPMG Australia, with funding from KPMG International, KPMG Australia, and the University of Melbourne.\\n\\n\\n\\nThis study offers a data-driven view of where leadership focus, strategic investment and workforce enablement are most urgently needed and how rising public expectations for strong AI regulation and governance need to be addressed.\\n\\nData was collected in each country using representative research panels. Panel members were invited to complete the survey online, with data collected between November 2024 and mid-January 2025. The University of Melbourne research team, led by Professor Nicole Gillespie and Dr Steve Lockey, led the design, conduct, data collection, analysis, and reporting of this research. To cite this research and for full research findings and method details: Gillespie, N., Lockey, S., Ward, T., Macdade, A., & Hassed, G. (2025). Trust, Attitudes and Use of Artificial Intelligence: A Global Study 2025. The University of Melbourne and KPMG. \\n\\nDOI 10.26188/28822919 \\u00b7 This research was supported by the Chair in Trust research partnership between the University of Melbourne and KPMG Australia, with funding from KPMG International, KPMG Australia, and the University of Melbourne.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://kpmg.com/xx/en/our-insights/ai-and-technology/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai.html\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"May 1, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Trust in AI emerges from distrust in humans: A machine learning study on decision-making guidance\", \"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.16769\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"arxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"arxiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Fifty-five undergraduate students participated in an experiment involving 30 decision-making scenarios (factual, emotional, moral), selecting from AI agents (e.g., ChatGPT), voice assistants, peers, adults, or priests as guides. Data were analyzed using K-Modes and K-Means clustering for patterns, and XGBoost models with SHAP interpretations to predict AI selection based on sociodemographic and prior trust variables.\\n\\nResults showed adults (35.05%) and AI (28.29%) as the most selected agents overall. Clustering revealed context-specific preferences: AI dominated factual scenarios, while humans prevailed in social/moral ones. Lower prior trust in human agents (priests, peers, adults) consistently predicted higher AI selection, supporting deferred trust as a compensatory transfer.\\n\\nParticipant profiles with higher AI trust were distinguished by human distrust, lower technology use, and higher socioeconomic status. \\n\\nPoor trust and trustworthiness calibration in artificial intelligence can lead to maladaptive interactions with these technologies (Hancock et al., 2020; Mudit, Siddhant, & Subbarao, 2024; Razin & Alexander, 2024; Sap, LeBras, Fried, & Choi, 2022). Adult participants report perceiving AI-supported decision making as less fair, particularly when outcomes are unfavorable to them, while biases also emerge in children's views of artificial agents' competence compared to human agents; children tend to perceive that artificial agents do not make errors even when both face equal epistemic competition and prefer them in guidance situations (Bedemariam & Wessel, 2023; Stower, Kappas, & Sommer, 2024a).\\n\\n\\n\\nThose factors can be enumerated as higher perceptions of anthropomorphism, perceived cognitive competencies of AI agent, scenarios with risk for participants or others, knowledge background required for decision making, the declaration of intentionalities of AI agents, personality of AI agent and reputation of the technology (Capiola et al., 2023; Epley, 2018; Fahn\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.16769\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Trust in AI | Global insights 2025\", \"url\": \"https://kpmg.com/au/en/insights/artificial-intelligence-ai/trust-in-ai-global-insights-2025.html\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"kpmg.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=kpmg.com\", \"site_name\": \"KPMG\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Global survey results exploring people\\u2019s trust in AI, responsible AI, expectations of AI governance, and benefits and risks for business and society.\\n\\nThe research was conducted using an online survey completed by representative research panels in each country between November 2024 and mid-January 2025.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://kpmg.com/au/en/insights/artificial-intelligence-ai/trust-in-ai-global-insights-2025.html\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"April 23, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Trust in large language model-based solutions in healthcare among people with and without diabetes: a cross-sectional survey from the Health in Central Denmark cohort\", \"url\": \"https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.02.24.25322734.full.pdf\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"medrxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=medrxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"medrxiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"The statistical analyses were performed using the R statistical computing environment (R Foundation for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria; version 4.1) and we used the waffle, nnet and dplyr packages to conduct analyses and for illustrations.\\n\\n## Results\\n\\n**Table 1: tbl1:** \\n\\nFrom the invited population, 39,109 participants (29 % response rate) completed the AI-related questions in the HICD 2024 survey ([Fig fig1]). The mean (SD) age of the respondents was 63 years (SD: 11), with 46% being women. 60% did not have diabetes, 4% had type 1 diabetes, and 36% had type 2 diabetes. Full demographic details and questionnaire responses, shown by diabetes status, are presented in [Table tbl1]. A higher proportion of participants had more than 15 years of education (29%) compared to non-responders (20%) (Table S2).\\n\\n## Awareness and use of ChatGPT and AI\\n\\nMost responders (76%) had heard of ChatGPT, and 8,166 individuals (21%) had tried or used the application.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.02.24.25322734.full.pdf\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}], \"is_error\": false, \"structured_content\": null, \"meta\": null, \"message\": null, \"integration_name\": null, \"mcp_server_url\": null, \"integration_icon_url\": null, \"icon_name\": \"globe\", \"display_content\": null}, {\"start_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T10:12:06.901998Z\", \"stop_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T10:12:07.530263Z\", \"flags\": null, \"type\": \"tool_use\", \"id\": \"toolu_01UyS9HHQgKPcYmScEjz39Tu\", \"name\": \"web_search\", \"input\": {\"query\": \"Workday Hanover Research AI time saved rework correction 40 percent report 2024\"}, \"message\": \"Searching the web\", \"integration_name\": null, \"integration_icon_url\": null, \"icon_name\": \"globe\", \"context\": null, \"display_content\": null, \"approval_options\": null, \"approval_key\": null, \"is_mcp_app\": null, \"mcp_server_url\": null}, {\"start_timestamp\": null, \"stop_timestamp\": null, \"flags\": null, \"type\": \"tool_result\", \"tool_use_id\": \"toolu_01UyS9HHQgKPcYmScEjz39Tu\", \"name\": \"web_search\", \"content\": [{\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"New Workday Research: Companies Are Leaving AI Gains on the Table | Workday\", \"url\": \"https://investor.workday.com/news-and-events/press-releases/news-details/2026/New-Workday-Research-Companies-Are-Leaving-AI-Gains-on-the-Table/default.aspx\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"workday.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=workday.com\", \"site_name\": \"Workday\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"... Nearly 40% of AI time savings are lost to rework, including correcting errors, rewriting content, and verifying outputs from one-size-fits-all AI tools. Only 14% of employees consistently get clear, positive net outcomes from AI.\\n\\nWorkday, Inc (NASDAQ: WDAY) today released new global research showing that while AI is delivering productivity gains, many organizations aren't fully capturing its value. Employees are saving meaningful time with AI tools, but too often those gains are being absorbed by rework \\u2013 fixing mistakes, rewriting content, and double-checking outputs from generic tools \\u2013 leaving significant value on the table. The report, \\\" Beyond Productivity: Measuring the Real Value of AI ,\\\" reveals what separates leaders from laggards: the most successful organizations don't just deploy AI \\u2013 they reinvest the time it saves into their people. \\n\\nThe study encompassed 3,200 respondents across North America; Asia-Pacific (APAC); and Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (EMEA). All participants were full-time employees at organizations with $100M+ in annual revenue and were active users of AI technology.\\n\\nAI is delivering meaningful time savings, but that speed doesn't always translate into better outcomes. While 85% of employees report saving one to seven hours per week using AI, much of that time is offset by rework on low\\u2011quality AI\\u2011generated content \\u2013 creating a false sense of productivity and ROI. AI is doing its part by increasing capacity \\u2013 but too often, roles, skills, and processes haven't evolved to turn that capacity into consistently better results. ... Nearly 40% of AI time savings are lost to rework, including correcting errors, rewriting content, and verifying outputs from one-size-fits-all AI tools. The biggest opportunity is helping employees learn how to use AI effectively\\u2014especially in areas that require judgement, creativity, and decision-making. About the Report This data comes from the global study \\u201cBeyond Productivity: Measuring the Real Value of AI,\\u201d a survey conducted by Workday and fielded by Hanover Research in November 2025.
