Frequent users feel the most strain: Employees who use AI every day are overwhelmingly optimistic \\u2013 more than 90% believe it will help them succeed.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://en-gb.newsroom.workday.com/2026-01-14-New-Workday-Research-Companies-Are-Leaving-AI-Gains-on-the-Table\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Almost half of the time saved using AI is spent correcting outputs | CFO.com\", \"url\": \"https://www.cfo.com/news/almost-half-of-time-saved-using-ai-is-spent-correcting-outputs-cfo-ai-use-errors-workday-report-/810018/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"cfo.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=cfo.com\", \"site_name\": \"CFO.com\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"By Workday\\u2019s calculations, 37% of the time employees saved using AI tools was lost to \\u201crework,\\u201d which the company defined as \\u201ccorrecting, clarifying or rewriting low-quality AI-generated content.\\u201d \\u00b7 \\u201cFor every 10 hours of efficiency ...\\n\\nThough 85% of the workers surveyed said they\\u2019re saving between one and seven hours a week using AI tools, they\\u2019re also losing some of that time correcting or validating the technology\\u2019s outputs. By Workday\\u2019s calculations, 37% of the time employees saved using AI tools was lost to \\u201crework,\\u201d which the company defined as \\u201ccorrecting, clarifying or rewriting low-quality AI-generated content.\\u201d \\u00b7 \\u201cFor every 10 hours of efficiency gained through AI, nearly 4 hours are lost to fixing its output,\\u201d Workday officials wrote in the report. The company described this as the \\u201cAI tax on productivity.\\u201d \\u00b7 Workday surveyed a total of 3,200 respondents, with half comprising leaders and the other half employees.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.cfo.com/news/almost-half-of-time-saved-using-ai-is-spent-correcting-outputs-cfo-ai-use-errors-workday-report-/810018/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"January 21, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"AI Productivity Paradox: 40% of Time Savings Lost to Rework\", \"url\": \"https://www.newkerala.com/news/a/nearly-40-ai-time-savings-lost-rework-report-913.htm\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"newkerala.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=newkerala.com\", \"site_name\": \"New Kerala\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"While the study, titled &quot;Beyond Productivity: Measuring the Real Value of AI,&quot; found that 85% of employees report saving between one and seven hours per week using AI tools, nearly 40% of those time savings are currently being lost to rework.\\n\\nNew global research released by Workday reveals a significant \\\"productivity paradox\\\" where organizations are failing to capture the full value of artificial intelligence. While the study, titled \\\"Beyond Productivity: Measuring the Real Value of AI,\\\" found that 85% of employees report saving between one and seven hours per week using AI tools, nearly 40% of those time savings are currently being lost to rework. This loss stems from employees having to fix mistakes, rewrite content, and verify outputs from generic AI tools, creating what the report describes as a \\\"false sense of productivity\\\". The burden of this rework is not distributed evenly across the workforce. \\n\\nAccording to the data, employees aged 25-34 bear the heaviest load, making up 46% of those dealing with the highest levels of AI correction. Furthermore, those who use the technology most frequently are under the most strain; 77% of daily AI users report reviewing AI-generated work just as carefully as, or even more carefully\\n\\nGerrit Kazmaier, president of product and technology at Workday, noted that \\\"too many AI tools push the hard questions of trust, accuracy, and repeatability back onto individual users\\\". He emphasized that the goal should be for AI to handle the \\\"complex work under the hood\\\" so that employees can focus on judgment and creativity. The research indicates that the most successful organizations treat saved time as a strategic resource, with employees who see positive outcomes being far more likely to have received increased skills training and to use their extra time for strategic thinking rather than just taking on more tasks. ... \\n\\nThe stat about 89% of organizations not updating job roles is the real issue. We're told to use AI to be\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.newkerala.com/news/a/nearly-40-ai-time-savings-lost-rework-report-913.htm\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"January 15, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Workday Study: 40% of AI Time Savings Lost to Rework - UC Today\", \"url\": \"https://www.uctoday.com/productivity-automation/workday-reveals-why-most-companies-are-leaving-ai-value-on-the-table/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"uctoday.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=uctoday.com\", \"site_name\": \"UC Today\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Stop measuring vanity metrics. \\u201cHours saved\\u201d means nothing if half that time disappears into correction and verification. Measure net productivity: time saved minus time spent on rework. Close the training gap. The 30-point disconnect between leadership intent and employee experience won\\u2019t fix itself. Provide hands-on training in real work contexts, not theoretical overviews. Teach judgment frameworks for when to use AI and when to walk away. Redesign work, not just workflows. Update job descriptions to reflect what AI can actually do. Redefine roles around judgment, creativity, and strategic thinking. Remove legacy processes that create friction with modern tools. Choose tools that carry their weight. AI should reduce user burden, not increase it. Evaluate platforms based on built-in accuracy mechanisms, context-awareness, and transparency about limitations.\\n\\n\\n\\nUC Today delivers insights for IT leaders and buyers covering Agentic AI, Agentic AI in the Workplace\\u200b, AI Agents, AI Copilots & Assistants\\u200b, Artificial Intelligence, Chatbots, Copilot, Employee Experience, Generative AI, Low-Code Automation\\u200b, Productivity, Virtual Agents for IT (AIOps)\\u200b, collaboration, employee experience and workspace tech.\\n\\nNew Workday research reveals the \\\"AI tax\\\" costing businesses productivity gains, and why digital natives are bearing the biggest burden ... Your team is using AI. Employees are enthusiastic. Hours are being saved. So why isn\\u2019t productivity actually improving? New research from Workday has uncovered what the company is calling \\u201cthe AI tax\\u201d, and it\\u2019s costing businesses more than they realize. For every 10 hours employees save using AI tools, nearly four hours vanish into fixing errors and fact-checking outputs that can\\u2019t be trusted. \\n\\nThe study of 3,200 employees across global enterprises found that 85% report saving one to seven hours per week with AI, yet only 14% consistently achieve positive net outcomes. The difference is what Workday terms\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.uctoday.com/productivity-automation/workday-reveals-why-most-companies-are-leaving-ai-value-on-the-table/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"January 14, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"AI Exposed: New Research Reveals AI Increases Rework By 40%, Leaving Employees Working Longer Hours, Spending More Time Fixing Errors\", \"url\": \"https://www.newsx.com/tech-and-auto/ai-exposed-new-research-reveals-ai-increases-rework-by-40-leaving-employees-working-longer-hours-spending-more-time-fixing-errors-148326/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"newsx.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=newsx.com\", \"site_name\": \"NewsX\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"According to the study \\u201cBeyond Productivity: Measuring the Real Value of AI,\\u201d 85% of workers say they use AI technologies to save one to seven hours a week; nevertheless, rework currently accounts for approximately 40% of these time saves.\\n\\nAccording to recent worldwide data published by Workday, there is a serious \\u201cproductivity paradox\\u201d where businesses are not fully utilizing artificial intelligence. According to the study \\u201cBeyond Productivity: Measuring the Real Value of AI,\\u201d 85% of workers say they use AI technologies to save one to seven hours a week; nevertheless, rework currently accounts for approximately 40% of these time saves. The research characterizes this loss as a \\u201cfalse sense of productivity\\u201d caused by employees having to correct errors, rewrite content, and validate outputs from generic AI technologies. ... Katy Perry\\u2019s Wild Love Life: Sexy Boyfriends, Messy Breakups, \\u2018Dirty\\u2019 Songs- Here\\u2019s the Full Story ... \\n\\nThe workforce is not equally burdened by this rework. Employees between the ages of 25 and 34 have the greatest burden, accounting for 46% of those who deal with the highest levels of AI correction, according to the statistics.\\n\\n\\u201cToo many AI tools push the hard questions of trust, accuracy, and repeatability back onto individual users,\\u201d said Gerrit Kazmaier, head of product and technology at Workday. He underlined that the objective should be for AI to take care of the \\u201ccomplex work under the hood\\u201d so that workers may concentrate on their creativity and judgment. According to the research, the most successful companies view time savings as a strategic resource. Employees who witness favorable results are much more likely to have received additional skill training and to use their free time for strategic thinking as opposed to simply taking on more work. ... Only 14% of employees consistently report net-positive outcomes from AI use, according to the report.\\n\\nWorkday conducted the study, \\u201cBeyond Productivity: Measuring the Real Value of AI,\\u201d with Hanover Research, based on a survey of 3,200 employees and business leaders. They found that nearly 40% of the time saved through AI is offset by time spent correcting, verifying, or rewriting low-quality outputs. Only 14% of employees consistently report net-positive outcomes from AI use, according to the report. The findings suggest that while AI adoption is widespread and often accelerates task completion, organizations are measuring success in ways that challenge expectations regarding AI\\u2019s utility. \\n\\nThe report centers on what it calls an \\u201cAI tax on productivity.\\u201d \\u00b7 Workday\\u2019s research found that roughly 37% of the time employees save using AI is lost to rework, including correcting errors, verifying outputs, and rewriting content that fails to meet quality or context requirements.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://erp.today/workday-research-finds-ai-productivity-gains-are-lost-to-rework/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"January 17, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Categories: Tech and Auto\", \"url\": \"https://www.newsx.com/tech-and-auto/ai-exposed-new-research-reveals-ai-increases-rework-by-40-leaving-employees-working-longer-hours-spending-more-time-fixing-errors-148326/amp\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"newsx.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=newsx.com\", \"site_name\": \"newsx\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Categories: Tech and Auto\\n\\nAI Exposed: New Research Reveals AI Increases Rework By 40%, Leaving Employees Working Longer Hours, Spending More Time Fixing Errors\\n\\nWorkday\\u2019s study shows that nearly 40% of time saved by AI is lost to rework, as employees fix errors from generic AI tools\\u2014creating a false sense of productivity.\\n\\nAdd NewsX As A Trusted Source\\nAdd as a preferred\\nsource on Google\\nPublished by NewsX WebDesk\\nLast updated: January 15, 2026 17:17:18 IST\\n\\nAccording to recent worldwide data published by Workday, there is a serious \\u201cproductivity paradox\\u201d where businesses are not fully utilizing artificial intelligence. We never use customer content\\u2014such as information within an email, chat, document, or meeting\\u2014to produce reports. Our goal is to discover and share broad workplace trends that are anonymized by aggregating the data broadly from those trillions of signals that make up the Microsoft Graph.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/worklab/work-trend-index\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Agents, human agency, and the opportunity for organizations\", \"url\": \"https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/worklab/work-trend-index/agents-human-agency-and-the-opportunity-for-every-organization\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"microsoft.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=microsoft.com\", \"site_name\": \"Microsoft\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"See \\u201cAI Impact Analysis\\u201d in methodology for more details. 9 Organizational &amp; individual factors: Based on composite indices capturing respondents\\u2019 perceptions of AI culture, manager support, and talent practices, as well as their own AI mindset, each derived from multiple survey items assessing behaviors, attitudes, and workplace conditions related to AI use. See methodology for more details. All data is based on aggregated and anonymized Microsoft 365 productivity signals. Prompts per User: Number of prompts per user in a rolling 28-day period. Includes all countries and customer segments (including EU). Excludes government industry. Data spans March 2025 through March 2026.\\n\\nSee \\u201cAI Impact Analysis\\u201d in methodology for more details. 9 Organizational & individual factors: Based on composite indices capturing respondents\\u2019 perceptions of AI culture, manager support, and talent practices, as well as their own AI mindset, each derived from multiple survey items assessing behaviors, attitudes, and workplace conditions related to AI use. See \\u201cAI Impact Analysis\\u201d in methodology for more details. 10 Year over year change in the count of unique active agents in the Microsoft 365 Copilot Agents platform and SharePoint in a rolling 28-day period. See methodology for more details. All data is based on aggregated and anonymized Microsoft 365 productivity signals. Prompts per User: Number of prompts per user in a rolling 28-day period. Includes all countries and customer segments (including EU). Excludes government industry. Data spans March 2025 through March 2026.\\n\\nThis analysis examines the evolving role of managers in AI-first organizations. The Microsoft People Science team analyzed survey responses from 1,800 employees globally as part of the Microsoft People Science Agentic Teaming & Trust Survey (July 2025), including 819 leaders (C-suite, VP, Director), 520 managers, and 461 individual contributors. The Work Trend Index survey was conducted by an independent research firm, Edelman Data x Intelligence, among 20,000 full-time employed or self-employed knowledge workers who use AI at work across 10 markets between February 18, 2026, and April 7, 2026. This survey was 20 minutes in length and conducted online, in either the English language or translated into a local language across markets. 2,000 full-time workers were surveyed in each market, and global results have been aggregated across all responses to provide an average.\\n\\nMicrosoft 365 Copilot telemetry, one week in February 2026. Percentages show each activity\\u2019s share of classified Copilot user goals\\u2014not time spent, not session count. Each interaction is classified by user intent against the O*NET Intermediate Work Activities taxonomy and then mapped to Generalized Work Activities (GWA) (underlying filter: IWA share \\u2265 0.05% (Counts, S., Chen, Y., Dong, J., Sharma, H., Zaikin, A., Hu, R., Kok, A., and Ozer Yilmaz, G. (2025). \\u201dHow people use M365 Copilot Chat.\\u201d Microsoft Working Paper.) and grouped into the four O*NET Work Activity Categories: 4.A.1 Information Input, 4.A.2 Mental Processes, 4.A.3 Work Output, 4.A.4 Interacting With Others ... Employees at every level now have a partner that helps them analyze, synthesize, and deepen their own expertise, while also building expertise in other areas. AI is not just helping us do things faster.\\n\\nMicrosoft 365 Copilot agent telemetry | March 2025 through March 2026 | All metrics expressed as shares and ratios. No absolute counts. ... For example, Frontier Professionals are more likely than non\\u2013Frontier Professionals to say their teams brainstorm and refine business processes together to identify AI opportunities (63% vs. 32%), share AI tips, new agents, learnings, and mistakes (61% vs. 36%), and discuss quality standards for AI-assisted work (54% vs. 29%). They are also more likely to report that agent workflows, human handoffs, and quality standards are documented and repeatable at the team (26% vs. 19%), function (29% vs. 17%), and organization level (25% vs. 14%). Creating those systems requires a disciplined approach to holding humans accountable for the work that agents execute. Many functions that deploy agents at scale will start to see a pattern: the more agents execute, the higher the stakes around human evaluation.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/worklab/work-trend-index/agents-human-agency-and-the-opportunity-for-every-organization\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"2 weeks ago\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Microsoft New Future of Work Report 2025 A summary of recent research from\", \"url\": \"https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/New-Future-Of-Work-Report-2025.pdf\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"microsoft.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=microsoft.com\", \"site_name\": \"Microsoft\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"research consistently shows that when workers\\u2019 expertise and perspectives inform the design and deployment of workplace \\u00b7 technologies, organizations achieve more sustainable improvements in productivity and well-being (Trist &amp; Bamforth, 1951; Roethlisberger &amp; Dickson, 1939; Hackman &amp; Oldham, 1976). ... Ethnographic and HCI research demonstrates that workers adapt technology in creative ways, and that participatory design\\u2014 \\u00b7 where workers are co-designers\\u2014results in tools that better fit real workflows and foster higher adoption (Suchman, 1987; Orr, 1996; Awumey et al., 2024; Ehn, 1993; Doellgast et al., 2025). ... Combining technical and social science research methods can create AI systems that improve worker skills and satisfaction\\u2014 \\u00b7 not just accuracy\\u2014by embedding human-centric metrics, workers\\u2019 values, and skill-building into their design (Bucinca, 2025).\\n\\nAI products that integrate human thinking, creativity, and expertise while amplifying their value can promote adoption without \\u00b7 raising concerns about replacement (Ali et al., 2025; Young et al., 2025; Sharma, 2025). For example, an AI assistant can act as a \\u00b7 thought partner, helping users explore ideas and connect concepts across their knowledge base. ... Some of the best ways an organization might find to use AI \\u201ccome from the edge, not the center\\u201d (Winsor, 2024). Organizations can facilitate AI adoption by creating systems and incentives for employees to share how they use AI with one \\u00b7 another (Tursunbayeva & Chalutz-Ben Gal, 2024; Winsor, 2024). ... Employees are more likely to experiment with using AI and to share those insights with others when they feel safe and trust \\u00b7 their organizations (Tursunbayeva & Chalutz-Ben Gal, 2024; Bankins et al., 2021).\\n\\nMany employees, particularly Gen X, will not adopt tools that make them conform to a way of working - they want products \\u00b7 that are flexible enough to fit personal ways of working (Rozsa et al., 2023; Doblinger, 2023). Frontier Firms aim to integrate AI as a core asset rather than an experiment. The Index highlights a growing gap between expectations and worker capacity: Business leaders are driving AI ...\\n\\nKey findings from the 2025 Microsoft Work Trend Index: the rise of frontier firms, AI adoption in workplaces, hybrid human\\u2011AI teams, workforce dynamics and emerging career trends.\\n\\nThe 2025 Work Trend Index is Microsoft\\u2019s latest annual report analysing global workplace shifts, based on survey data, Microsoft 365 productivity signals and LinkedIn labour market trends. It outlines how AI, workforce capacity challenges and new organisational models are reshaping how people work. Official source: 2025 Microsoft Work Trend Index report \\u00b7 One of the main conclusions is the birth of the \\u201cFrontier Firm\\u201d \\u2014 an organisation structured around on\\u2011demand intelligence and powered by hybrid teams of humans and AI agents. These firms operate with agility, scale faster, and blend human expertise with AI workflows to drive innovation and performance. ... ~82 % plan to use digital labour (AI agents) to expand workforce capacity in the next 12\\u201318 months. Frontier Firms aim to integrate AI as a core asset rather than an experiment. The Index highlights a growing gap between expectations and worker capacity: Business leaders are driving AI adoption faster than employees.\\n\\nProductivity and capacity pressures are central challenges for modern work. Leaders are more familiar with AI than the wider workforce. Work dynamics are shifting from rigid hierarchies to adaptive \\u201cwork charts\\u201d. Different regions reflect similar themes with local nuances \\u2014 for example, in Ireland, a majority of workers see AI skills as key to career growth, highlighting a global appetite for new skills and adaptability in the workplace. The 2025 Work Trend Index signals that the future of work is deeply tied to AI adoption, human\\u2011AI collaboration, and organisational evolution. Staying updated with trends, embracing new skills and understanding hybrid workflows will be crucial for both workers and leaders as businesses transform. \\ud83d\\udccd The Work Trend Index is a mix of survey insights and real usage data, offering a practical picture of what\\u2019s shaping work in 2025 and beyond \\u2014 from frontier firms to daily workflows enhanced by AI.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://dir.md/support.microsoft.com/annual-work-trend-index-2025/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"AI at Work Is Here. Now Comes the Hard Part\", \"url\": \"https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/worklab/work-trend-index/ai-at-work-is-here-now-comes-the-hard-part\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"microsoft.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=microsoft.com\", \"site_name\": \"Microsoft\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"75% of knowledge workers use AI ... ago. It\\u2019s paying off: Users say AI helps them save time (90%), focus on their most important work (85%), be more creative (84%), and enjoy their work more (83%)....\\n\\nTo help leaders and organizations overcome AI inertia, Microsoft and LinkedIn looked at how AI will reshape work and the labor market broadly, surveying 31,000 people across 31 countries, identifying labor and hiring trends from LinkedIn, and analyzing trillions of Microsoft 365 productivity signals as well as research with Fortune 500 customers. The data points to insights every leader and professional needs to know\\u2014and actions they can take\\u2014when it comes to AI\\u2019s implications for work. Download the executive summaryDownload the full report \\u00b7 1. Employees want AI at work\\u2014and they won\\u2019t wait for companies to catch up. Already, AI is being woven into the workplace at an unexpected scale. 75% of knowledge workers use AI at work today, and 46% of users started using it less than six months ago. It\\u2019s paying off: Users say AI helps them save time (90%), focus on their most important work (85%), be more creative (84%), and enjoy their work more (83%).\\n\\n6 Unpublished data from LinkedIn\\u2019s Executive Confidence Index \\u00b7 7 Unpublished data from LinkedIn\\u2019s 2024 Workplace Learning Report ... The Work Trend Index survey was conducted by an independent research firm, Edelman Data & Intelligence, among 31,000 full-time employed or self-employed knowledge workers across 31 markets between February 15, 2024 and March 28, 2024. This survey was 20 minutes in length and conducted online, in either the English language or translated into a local language across markets. One thousand full-time workers were surveyed in each market, and global results have been aggregated across all responses to provide an average. In the US, an additional sample of 2,800 full-time employed or self-employed knowledge workers was collected across nine sub-regions/metros. Global markets surveyed include: Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Colombia, Czech Republic, Finland, France, Germany, Hong Kong, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Malaysia, Mexico, Netherlands,\\n\\nLinkedIn\\u2019s Executive Confidence Index (ECI) is an online survey taken every quarter by ~5,000 LinkedIn members (at the VP level or above). The most recent wave ran from March 4\\u201319, 2024. Members are randomly sampled and must be opted into research to participate. We analyze data in aggregate and will always respect member privacy. Data is weighted by Seniority and Industry to ensure fair representation of executives on the platform. The results represent the world as seen through the lens of LinkedIn\\u2019s membership; variances between LinkedIn\\u2019s membership and the overall market population are not accounted for. ... The LinkedIn Learning 2024 Workplace Learning Report surveyed 1,636 L&D and HR professionals with L&D responsibilities who have some influence on budget decisions, and 1,063 learners. Surveyed geographies include: North America (United States, Canada); South America (Brazil); Asia-Pacific (Australia, New Zealand, India, Japan, Cambodia, Indonesia, Singapore, Malaysia, Myanmar,\\n\\n\\u201cOver the past few decades, companies have been renegotiating the psychological contract\\u2014the why of work\\u2014with their employees, influenced by new generations, labor trends, and the pandemic. Now companies must renegotiate the \\u2018operational contract\\u2019\\u2014the how of work\\u2014with their employees as AI puts more power into the hands of workers in terms of the way the job gets done.\\u201d \\u00b7 \\u2014Constance Noonan Hadley, Organizational Psychologist, Institute for Life at Work and Boston University Questrom School of Business \\u00b7 While leaders recognize the value of bringing on new employees with AI aptitude, they\\u2019re missing the value of developing their own people: 45% of US executives are not currently investing in AI tools or products for employees.6 \\u00b7 Only 39% of people globally who use AI at work have gotten AI training from their company. Only 25% of companies are planning to offer training on generative AI this year, further cementing this training deficit.7 ...\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/worklab/work-trend-index/ai-at-work-is-here-now-comes-the-hard-part\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Leading the AI revolution: Insights from Microsoft's Work Trend Index - Microsoft in Business Blogs\", \"url\": \"https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/industry/microsoft-in-business/future-of-work/2025/04/25/leading-the-ai-revolution-insights-from-microsofts-work-trend-index/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"microsoft.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=microsoft.com\", \"site_name\": \"Microsoft\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Research shows that individuals with AI perform better than teams without it, but teams with AI outperform them all. Getting this balance right will reshape organizational charts and boost productivity, but doing it successfully requires ongoing ...\\n\\nMicrosoft has released its annual Work Trend Index, uncovering a profound shift redefining how organizations operate. This comprehensive report combines data from Microsoft 365 productivity signals, LinkedIn labor trends, and insights from Fortune 500 companies, AI-native startups, economists, scientists, and academics\\u2014offering a clear view of the evolving workplace and what it means for business leaders moving forward. 85% of business leaders across the Eastern U.S. believe this is a pivotal year to rethink core aspects of strategy and operations. Many believe AI agents will play a critical role in their evolution with 82% planning to use agents to expand their workforce capacity in the next 12-18 months. With AI agents that operate around the clock, scale instantly, and are cost-effective, Frontier Firms\\u2014companies that employ a combination of human and AI agent teams\\u2014can significantly enhance productivity, performance and morale to gain a competitive edge and position themselves as i\\n\\nThese AI agents can handle tasks, drive workflows, and keep working around the clock. Start by identifying teams that can benefit most from AI agents to boost both individual and team productivity. Leaders in the Eastern U.S. were surveyed and welcomed this added 24/7 support. 50% of respondents said their organization is using AI agents to automate workstreams or business processes for entire teams or functions. The top reason employees turned to AI over a colleague is its 24/7 availability (47%)\\u2014followed by speed and quality of work (29%) and the provision of unlimited creative ideas (23%). Sales, Operations, and Finance are evolving into \\u201cagent-first\\u201d functions, where AI handles the heavy lifting while HR, Strategy, and other decision-making roles remain \\u201chuman-first.\\u201d This new structure will enable agile, fluid human-AI teams to produce high-value work efficiently.\\n\\nResearch shows that individuals with AI perform better than teams without it, but teams with AI outperform them all. Getting this balance right will reshape organizational charts and boost productivity, but doing it successfully requires ongoing attention. Employees drive business impact; however, our survey found that employees continue to feel stretched thin and drained from low-value tasks like email triage or invoice review. Adding agents to your teams can offload routine tasks to AI, empowering employees to focus on more creative and strategic work. This shift has the potential to reshape entire industries and transform collective productivity. Husch Blackwell\\u2014a law firm serving the energy sector\\u2014is a strong example of this. When they experienced a significant increase in cases, they needed a solution to help lawyers increase efficiency, reduce costs, and improve relationships by aiding in the completion of routine tasks.\\n\\nWhile 73% of leaders are familiar or extremely familiar with AI agents, only 45% of employees say the same. 50% of managers expect AI training or upskilling to become a key responsibility for their teams in the next five years. Last year, we helped train and certify more than 23 million people around the world in digital skills. We\\u2019re excited about our new collaboration with Pearson to expand on this by providing AI-powered products and services to help prepare the workforce for an AI-driven economy. To get started with establishing an AR team, check out our AI Skills navigator to provide your employees with a personalized learning path that suits their unique role and goals. The competition to secure AI-skilled talent is fierce. Read more in this year\\u2019s Work Trend Index to learn how you can leverage agents to handle routine tasks, empower your employees\\u2019 ingenuity, and position your business for success to stay ahead of the curve.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/industry/microsoft-in-business/future-of-work/2025/04/25/leading-the-ai-revolution-insights-from-microsofts-work-trend-index/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"April 25, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"II III 2025 Work Trend Index Annual Report Work Trend Index Annual Report\", \"url\": \"https://assets-c4akfrf5b4d3f4b7.z01.azurefd.net/assets/2025/04/2025_Work_Trend_Index_Annual_Report_680aaa7fe52dd.pdf\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"azurefd.net\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=azurefd.net\", \"site_name\": \"Azurefd\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"judgment, building systems that are AI-operated but \\u00b7 human-led. Like the Industrial Revolution and the \\u00b7 internet era, this transformation will take decades to \\u00b7 reach its full promise and involve broad technological, societal, and economic change. We also spoke with AI-native startups, academics, economists, scientists, and thought leaders to explore \\u00b7 what work could become. Structured around on-demand \\u00b7 intelligence and powered by \\u201chybrid\\u201d teams of \\u00b7 humans + agents, these companies scale rapidly, operate with agility, and generate value faster.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://assets-c4akfrf5b4d3f4b7.z01.azurefd.net/assets/2025/04/2025_Work_Trend_Index_Annual_Report_680aaa7fe52dd.pdf\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"April 24, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Work Trend Index | Will AI Fix Work?\", \"url\": \"https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/worklab/work-trend-index/will-ai-fix-work\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"microsoft.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=microsoft.com\", \"site_name\": \"Microsoft\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Amid fears of AI job loss, business ... \\u2018increasing employee productivity\\u2019 than \\u2018reducing headcount\\u2019 when asked what they would most value about AI in the workplace. Survey question: If the growth of artificial intelligence (AI) in the workplace could lead to the following benefits, which would add the most value to your workplace? Illustration by Manuel Bortoletti ... The data shows that business leaders are looking to empower people with AI rather than replace them\\u2014they\\u2019re 2x more interested in using AI to increase productivity than to cut headcount...\\n\\nAmid fears of AI job loss, business leaders are 2x more likely to choose \\u2018increasing employee productivity\\u2019 than \\u2018reducing headcount\\u2019 when asked what they would most value about AI in the workplace. Survey question: If the growth of artificial intelligence (AI) in the workplace could lead to the following benefits, which would add the most value to your workplace? Illustration by Manuel Bortoletti ... The data shows that business leaders are looking to empower people with AI rather than replace them\\u2014they\\u2019re 2x more interested in using AI to increase productivity than to cut headcount. In fact, reducing headcount was last on the list of what leaders would value from AI. After \\u201cincreasing productivity,\\u201d leaders\\u2019 top hopes for AI are to: help employees with necessary but repetitive tasks, increase employee wellbeing, eliminate employee time spent on low-value activities, enhance employees\\u2019 capabilities, and accelerate employees\\u2019 pace of work.\\n\\nAnd the tax on individual productivity is compounding, undermining organizational productivity and global GDP. AI can help lift the burden. To date, AI has mostly been on autopilot. Now, next-generation copilots will work alongside people, freeing us from digital debt and fueling innovation. Organizations that embrace AI will unleash creativity and unlock productivity for everyone\\u2014ushering in a new wave of productivity growth and value creation. \\u201cThis new generation of AI will remove the drudgery of work and unleash creativity,\\u201d said Satya Nadella, Chairman and CEO, Microsoft. \\u201cThere\\u2019s an enormous opportunity for AI-powered tools to help alleviate digital debt, build AI aptitude, and empower employees.\\u201d To ready leaders and businesses for the age of AI, we surveyed 31,000 people in 31 countries and analyzed trillions of Microsoft 365 productivity signals, along with labor trends from the LinkedIn Economic Graph.\\n\\nIn a world where creativity is the new productivity, digital debt is more than an inconvenience\\u2014it\\u2019s impacting business. With the balance of work hours spent communicating, 68% of people say they don\\u2019t have enough uninterrupted focus time during the workday. ... Hours worked, workday span, and time spent in meetings have steadily increased over the past few years\\u2014it\\u2019s easier than ever to communicate and harder than ever to keep up. Taking a closer look at how people spend their time, it\\u2019s clear that a lack of focus time, the search for information, and the volume of constant communications have an opportunity cost. Sixty-eight percent of people say they don\\u2019t have enough uninterrupted focus time during the workday. And 62% of survey respondents say they struggle with too much time spent searching for information in their workday. Across the Microsoft 365 apps, the average employee spends 57% of their time communicating (in meetings, email, and chat) and 43% creating (in documents, spre\\n\\nIdentify and address your organization\\u2019s productivity disruptors with insights from employee listening. Radically rethink the workday . As AI frees up time and energy, protect focus time for the creative work that leads to innovation. Think of meetings as a digital artifact and not just a point in time. Encourage people to leverage AI-powered intelligent meeting recaps, transcripts, and recordings to engage with meetings how and when it works best for them. ... Amid concerns of AI replacing jobs, the data revealed an unexpected insight: employees are more eager for AI to lift the weight of work than they are afraid of job loss to AI. While 49% of people say they\\u2019re worried AI will replace their jobs, even more\\u201470%\\u2014would delegate as much work as possible to AI to lessen their workloads. \\u201cIt\\u2019s fascinating that people are more excited about AI rescuing them from burnout than they are worried about it eliminating their jobs,\\u201d said author and organizational psychology professor Adam Grant.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/worklab/work-trend-index/will-ai-fix-work\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Microsoft\\u2019s 2025 Work Trend Index Report reveals the rise of the Frontier Firm, marking a new era of workforce dynamics - Source EMEA\", \"url\": \"https://news.microsoft.com/source/emea/features/microsofts-2025-work-trend-index-report-reveals-the-rise-of-the-frontier-firm-marking-a-new-era-of-workforce-dynamics/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"microsoft.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=microsoft.com\", \"site_name\": \"Microsoft EMEA\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Lastly, this year\\u2019s edition of the report spotlighted how more professionals are embracing AI agents as part of their roles, shifting towards a model where employees become agent bosses \\u2013 individuals who build, delegate to, and manage AI tools to enhance productivity. Leaders anticipate that within five years, teams will be regularly training and managing AI agents as part of their responsibilities. However, a gap in AI adoption remains, as 67% of leaders report familiarity with AI agents, compared to only 40% of employees. Furthermore, 79% of leaders believe AI will accelerate their careers, yet only 67% of employees share that optimism, highlighting an urgent need for AI education and upskilling. \\u201cThe findings of this year\\u2019s Work Trend Index make it clear that businesses must rethink how they harness AI to unlock their full potential. The rise of Frontier Firms demonstrates ...\\n\\nLastly, this year\\u2019s edition of the report spotlighted how more professionals are embracing AI agents as part of their roles, shifting towards a model where employees become agent bosses \\u2013 individuals who build, delegate to, and manage AI tools to enhance productivity. Leaders anticipate that within five years, teams will be regularly training and managing AI agents as part of their responsibilities. However, a gap in AI adoption remains, as 67% of leaders report familiarity with AI agents, compared to only 40% of employees. Furthermore, 79% of leaders believe AI will accelerate their careers, yet only 67% of employees share that optimism, highlighting an urgent need for AI education and upskilling. \\u201cThe findings of this year\\u2019s Work Trend Index make it clear that businesses must rethink how they harness AI to unlock their full potential. The rise of Frontier Firms demonstrates that leaders who strategically integrate AI-driven intelligence and empower human-agent teams will stay ahead i\\n\\nAzure Microsoft Developer Microsoft Learn Support for AI marketplace apps Microsoft Tech Community Microsoft Marketplace Marketplace Rewards Visual Studio \\u00b7 Microsoft Rewards Free downloads & security Education Gift cards Licensing Unlocked stories ... The report underscores how intelligence on demand, hybrid human-agent teams, and evolving workplace structures are reshaping business operations and talent strategies. 29 April 2025, Nairobi, Kenya \\u2013 As organizations worldwide navigate the next wave of workplace transformation, Microsoft\\u2019s fifth annual Work Trend Index (WTI) Report uncovers the emergence of a new kind of enterprise: the Frontier Firm. These businesses operate with intelligence on demand, leveraging hybrid human-agent teams to maximize efficiency and innovation. Conducted in partnership with LinkedIn, the report, titled \\u201c2025: The Year the Frontier Firm Is Born\\u201c, examines insights from 31,000 professionals across 31 countries, alongside LinkedIn \"approval_options\": null, \"approval_key\": null, \"is_mcp_app\": null, \"mcp_server_url\": null}, {\"start_timestamp\": null, \"stop_timestamp\": null, \"flags\": null, \"type\": \"tool_result\", \"tool_use_id\": \"toolu_01DBb8TrsYtvpnogAzBgkpqu\", \"name\": \"web_search\", \"content\": [{\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines \\u2013 Leonie Monigatti\", \"url\": \"https://www.leoniemonigatti.com/papers/dspy.html\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"leoniemonigatti.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=leoniemonigatti.com\", \"site_name\": \"Leonie Monigatti\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Although the paper [1] on the framework was already published in October 2023, I only recently learned about it. After just watching one video (\\u201cDSPy Explained!\\u201d by Connor Shorten), I could already understand why the developer community is so excited about DSPy! This article gives a brief introduction to the DSPy framework. DSPy (\\u201cDeclarative Self-improving Language Programs (in Python)\\u201c, pronounced\\u201ddee-es-pie\\u201d) [1] is a framework for \\u201cprogramming with foundation models\\u201d developed by researchers at Stanford NLP. It emphasizes programming over prompting and moves building LM-based pipelines away from manipulating prompts and closer to programming. Thus, it aims to solve the fragility problem in building LM-based applications. DSPy also provides a more systematic approach to building LM-based applications by separating the information flow of your program from the parameters (prompts and LM weights) of each step.\\n\\n\\n\\n[1] Khattab, O., Singhvi, A., Maheshwari, P., Zhang, Z., Santhanam, K., Vardhamanan, S., \\u2026 & Potts, C. (2023). Dspy: Compiling declarative language model calls into self-improving pipelines. arXiv preprint arXiv:2310.03714. I am a Developer Advocate at Weaviate at the time of this writing. This blog was originally published on Towards Data Science on Feb 27, 2024 and moved to this site on Oct 13, 2025.\\n\\nThe workflow of building an LM-based application with DSPy, as discussed in the DSPy Intro Notebook, is shown below. It will remind you of the workflow for training a neural network: ... Collect dataset: Collect a few examples of the inputs and outputs of your program (e.g., question and answer pairs), which will be used to optimize your pipeline. Write DSPy program: Define your program\\u2019s logic with signatures and modules and the information flow among the components to solve your task.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.leoniemonigatti.com/papers/dspy.html\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"February 27, 2024\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines | Fan Pu Zeng\", \"url\": \"https://fanpu.io/summaries/2024-03-15-dspy-compiling-declarative-language-model-calls-into-self-improving-pipelines/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"fanpu.io\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=fanpu.io\", \"site_name\": \"Fan Pu Zeng\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"On top of that, DSPy programs compiled to open and relatively small LMs like 770M-parameter T5 and llama2-13b-chat are competitive with approaches that rely on expert-written prompt chains for proprietary GPT-3.5. DSPy is available at https://github.com/stanfordnlp/dspy \\u00b7 @article{2310.03714v1, author = {Khattab, Omar and Singhvi, Arnav and Maheshwari, Paridhi and Zhang, Zhiyuan and Santhanam, Keshav and Vardhamanan, Sri and Haq, Saiful and Sharma, Ashutosh and Joshi, Thomas T. and Moazam, Hanna and Miller, Heather and Zaharia, Matei and Potts, Christopher}, title = {DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines}, eprint = {2310.03714v1}, archiveprefix = {arXiv}, primaryclass = {cs.CL}, year = {2023}, month = oct, url = {http://arxiv.org/abs/2310.03714v1}, file = {2310.03714v1.pdf}, eprintnover = {2310.03714} } From the authors of ColBERT, DSPy is a framework for improving a language model (LM) pipeline.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://fanpu.io/summaries/2024-03-15-dspy-compiling-declarative-language-model-calls-into-self-improving-pipelines/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"March 15, 2024\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"[2310.03714] DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines\", \"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.03714\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"arxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"arXiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"We design a compiler that will optimize any DSPy pipeline to maximize a given metric. We conduct two case studies, showing that succinct DSPy programs can express and optimize sophisticated LM pipelines that reason about math word problems, tackle multi-hop retrieval, answer complex questions, and control agent loops. Within minutes of compiling, a few lines of DSPy allow GPT-3.5 and llama2-13b-chat to self-bootstrap pipelines that outperform standard few-shot prompting (generally by over 25% and 65%, respectively) and pipelines with expert-created demonstrations (by up to 5-46% and 16-40%, respectively). On top of that, DSPy programs compiled to open and relatively small LMs like 770M-parameter T5 and llama2-13b-chat are competitive with approaches that rely on expert-written prompt chains for proprietary GPT-3.5. DSPy is available at this https URL \\u00b7 From: Omar Khattab [view email] [v1] Thu, 5 Oct 2023 17:37:25 UTC (77 KB) ...\\n\\n\\n\\nWe conduct two case studies, showing that succinct DSPy programs can express and optimize sophisticated LM pipelines that reason about math word problems, tac\\n\\nAbstract page for arXiv paper 2310.03714: DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines\\n\\nWe design a compiler that will optimize any DSPy pipeline to maximize a given metric. We conduct two case studies, showing that succinct DSPy programs can express and optimize sophisticated LM pipelines that reason about math word problems, tackle multi-hop retrieval, answer complex questions, and control agent loops. Within minutes of compiling, a few lines of DSPy allow GPT-3.5 and llama2-13b-chat to self-bootstrap pipelines that outperform standard few-shot prompting (generally by over 25% and 65%, respectively) and pipelines with expert-created demonstrations (by up to 5-46% and 16-40%, respectively). \\n\\nOn top of that, DSPy programs compiled to open and relatively small LMs like 770M-parameter T5 and llama2-13b-chat are competitive with approaches that rely\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.03714\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"October 5, 2023\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines\", \"url\": \"https://openreview.net/pdf?id=PFS4ffN9Yx\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"openreview.net\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=openreview.net\", \"site_name\": \"OpenReview\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"up-to-date documentation of the framework, we refer readers to https://github.com/stanfordnlp/dspy. 37th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2023). abstractions [Paszke et al., 2019], DSPy modules can be composed in imperative define-by-run \\u00b7 computational graphs, by using them in any logical control flow like if statements and loops. We \\u00b7 then develop the DSPy compiler (Sec 4), which optimizes any DSPy program to improve quality \\u00b7 or cost. Given a validation metric and some training inputs, the compiler simulates versions of the \\u00b7 program on the inputs and bootstraps example traces of each module for self-improvement, using them \\u00b7 to construct effective few-shot prompts or finetuning small LMs for steps of the pipeline. Optimization \\u00b7 in DSPy is conducted by teleprompters:2 generic optimization strategies that map declarative modules \\u00b7\\n\\nup-to-date documentation of the framework, we refer readers to https://github.com/stanfordnlp/dspy. \\n\\n37th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2023). abstractions [Paszke et al., 2019], DSPy modules can be composed in imperative define-by-run \\u00b7 computational graphs, by using them in any logical control flow like if statements and loops. We \\u00b7 then develop the DSPy compiler (Sec 4), which optimizes any DSPy program to improve quality \\u00b7 or cost. Given a validation metric and some training inputs, the compiler simulates versions of the \\u00b7 program on the inputs and bootstraps example traces of each module for self-improvement, using them \\u00b7 to construct effective few-shot prompts or finetuning small LMs for steps of the pipeline. Optimization \\u00b7 in DSPy is conducted by teleprompters:2 generic optimization strategies that map declarative modules \\u00b7\\n\\nUniquely, DSPy parameterizes these prompting techniques. \\n\\nTo understand this parameterization, observe that any LM call seeking to implement a particular signature needs to specify parameters \\u00b7 that include: (1) the specific LM to c\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://openreview.net/pdf?id=PFS4ffN9Yx\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"GitHub - stanfordnlp/dspy: DSPy: The framework for programming\\u2014not prompting\\u2014language models\", \"url\": \"https://github.com/stanfordnlp/dspy\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"github.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=github.com\", \"site_name\": \"GitHub\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"[Jul&#x27;25] GEPA: Reflective Prompt Evolution Can Outperform Reinforcement Learning [Jun&#x27;24] Optimizing Instructions and Demonstrations for Multi-Stage Language Model Programs [Oct&#x27;23] DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines [Jul&#x27;24] Fine-Tuning and Prompt Optimization: Two Great Steps that Work Better Together [Jun&#x27;24] Prompts as Auto-Optimized Training Hyperparameters [Feb&#x27;24] Assisting in Writing Wikipedia-like Articles From Scratch with Large Language Models [Jan&#x27;24] In-Context Learning for Extreme Multi-Label Classification [Dec&#x27;23] DSPy Assertions: Computational Constraints for Self-Refining Language Model Pipelines [Dec&#x27;22] Demonstrate-Search-Predict: Composing Retrieval &amp; Language Models for Knowledge-Intensive NLP \\u00b7 To stay up to date or learn more, follow @DSPyOSS on Twitter or the DSPy page on LinkedIn.\\n\\nDSPy stands for Declarative Self-improving Python. \\n\\n[Jul'25] GEPA: Reflective Prompt Evolution Can Outperform Reinforcement Learning [Jun'24] Optimizing Instructions and Demonstrations for Multi-Stage Language Model Programs [Oct'23] DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines [Jul'24] Fine-Tuning and Prompt Optimization: Two Great Steps that Work Better Together [Jun'24] Prompts as Auto-Optimized Training Hyperparameters [Feb'24] Assisting in Writing Wikipedia-like Articles From Scratch with Large Language Models [Jan'24] In-Context Learning for Extreme Multi-Label Classification [Dec'23] DSPy Assertions: Computational Constraints for Self-Refining Language Model Pipelines [Dec'22] Demonstrate-Search-Predict: Composing Retrieval & Language Models for Knowledge-Intensive NLP \\u00b7 To stay up to date or learn more, follow @DSPyOSS on Twitter or the DSPy page on LinkedIn.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://github.com/stanfordnlp/dspy\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"DSPy\", \"url\": \"https://dspy.ai/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"dspy.ai\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=dspy.ai\", \"site_name\": \"DSPy\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"The DSPy research effort started at Stanford NLP in Feb 2022, building on what we had learned from developing early compound LM systems like ColBERT-QA, Baleen, and Hindsight. The first version was released as DSP in Dec 2022 and evolved by Oct 2023 into DSPy. Thanks to 250 contributors, DSPy has introduced hundreds of thousands of people to building and optimizing modular LM programs. Since then, DSPy&#x27;s community has produced a large body of work on optimizers, like MIPROv2, BetterTogether, and LeReT, on program architectures, like STORM, IReRa, and DSPy Assertions, and on successful applications to new problems, like PAPILLON, PATH, WangLab@MEDIQA, UMD&#x27;s Prompting Case Study, and Haize&#x27;s Red-Teaming Program, in addition to many open-source projects, production applications, and other community work.\\n\\n\\n\\nThe DSPy research effort started at Stanford NLP in Feb 2022, building on what we had learned from developing early compound LM systems like ColBERT-QA, Baleen, and Hindsight. The first version was released as DSP in Dec 2022 and evolved by Oct 2023 into DSPy. Thanks to 250 contributors, DSPy has introduced hundreds of thousands of people to building and optimizing modular LM programs. Since then, DSPy's community has produced a large body of work on optimizers, like MIPROv2, BetterTogether, and LeReT, on program architectures, like STORM, IReRa, and DSPy Assertions, and on successful applications to new problems, like PAPILLON, PATH, WangLab@MEDIQA, UMD's Prompting Case Study, and Haize's Red-Teaming Program, in addition to many open-source projects, production applications, and other community work.\\n\\nDSPy is a declarative framework for building modular AI software.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://dspy.ai/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"[PDF] DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines | Semantic Scholar\", \"url\": \"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/DSPy:-Compiling-Declarative-Language-Model-Calls-Khattab-Singhvi/2069aaaa281eb13bcd9330fc4d43f24f6b436a53\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"semanticscholar.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=semanticscholar.org\", \"site_name\": \"Semantic Scholar\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"imperative computational graphs where LMs are invoked through declarative modules. DSPy modules are parameterized, meaning they can learn (by creating and collecting demonstrations) how to apply compositions of prompting, finetuning, augmen\\n\\n2024 \\u00b7 TLDR \\u00b7 This work proposes Optimization by PROmpting (OPRO), a simple and effective approach to leverage large language models (LLMs) as optimizers, where the optimization task is described in natural language.Expand \\u00b7 628[PDF] 2 Excerpts \\u00b7 Save \\u00b7 Qingyan GuoRui Wang+8 authors Microsoft Research \\u00b7 Computer Science \\u00b7 2023 \\u00b7 TLDR \\u00b7 EvoPrompt significantly outperforms human-engineered prompts and existing methods for automatic prompt generation and demonstrates that connecting LLMs with EAs creates synergies, which could inspire further research on the combination of LLMs and conventional algorithms.Expand \\u00b7 167[PDF] 1 Excerpt \\u00b7 Save \\u00b7 O. KhattabKeshav Santhanam+4 authors M. \\n\\nZaharia \\u00b7 Computer Science \\u00b7 ArXiv \\u00b7 2022 \\u00b7 TLDR \\u00b7 Demonstrate-Search-Predict (DSP) is proposed, a framework that relies on passing natural language texts in sophisticated pipelines between an LM and an RM and can express high-level programs that bootstrap pipeline-aware demonstrations, search for relevant passa\\n\\nDemonstrate-Search-Predict (DSP) is proposed, a framework that relies on passing natural language texts in sophisticated pipelines between an LM and an RM and can express high-level programs that bootstrap pipeline-aware demonstrations, search for relevant passages, and generate grounded predictions.Expand ... This work introduces a model selection method to combine the best of both worlds by employing a large language model (LLM) to dynamically select between them, and demonstrates significant performance improvements across eight reasoning datasets with Codex, ChatGPT, and GPT-4.Expand ... \\n\\nSynthetic prompting is introduced, a method that leverages a few handcrafted examples to prompt the model to generate more examples by i\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/DSPy:-Compiling-Declarative-Language-Model-Calls-Khattab-Singhvi/2069aaaa281eb13bcd9330fc4d43f24f6b436a53\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into ...\", \"url\": \"https://hai.stanford.edu/research/dspy-compiling-declarative-language-model-calls-into-state-of-the-art-pipelines\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"stanford.edu\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=stanford.edu\", \"site_name\": \"Stanford\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"We invited 11 sci-fi filmmakers and AI researchers to Stanford for Stories for the Future, a day-and-a-half experiment in fostering new narratives about AI.\\n\\nVafa et al. (2024) introduced a transformer-based econometric model, CAREER, that predicts a worker\\u2019s next job as a function of career history (an \\u201coccupation model\\u201d). CAREER was initially estimated (\\u201cpre-trained\\u201d) using a large, unrepresentative resume dataset, which served as a \\u201cfoundation model,\\u201d and parameter estimation was continued (\\u201cfine-tuned\\u201d) using data from a representative survey. CAREER had better predictive performance than benchmarks. This paper considers an alternative where the resume-based foundation model is replaced by a large language model (LLM). We convert tabular data from the survey into text files that resemble resumes and fine-tune the LLMs using these text files with the objective to predict the next token (word).\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://hai.stanford.edu/research/dspy-compiling-declarative-language-model-calls-into-state-of-the-art-pipelines\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}], \"is_error\": false, \"structured_content\": null, \"meta\": null, \"message\": null, \"integration_name\": null, \"mcp_server_url\": null, \"integration_icon_url\": null, \"icon_name\": \"globe\", \"display_content\": null}, {\"start_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T10:12:19.038281Z\", \"stop_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T10:12:19.414902Z\", \"flags\": null, \"type\": \"tool_use\", \"id\": \"toolu_01NUTpHQghnq59mkd2GjzaQb\", \"name\": \"web_search\", \"input\": {\"query\": \"LLM personalization system prompts custom instructions performance benchmark study 2024 2025\"}, \"message\": \"Searching the web\", \"integration_name\": null, \"integration_icon_url\": null, \"icon_name\": \"globe\", \"context\": null, \"display_content\": null, \"approval_options\": null, \"approval_key\": null, \"is_mcp_app\": null, \"mcp_server_url\": null}, {\"start_timestamp\": null, \"stop_timestamp\": null, \"flags\": null, \"type\": \"tool_result\", \"tool_use_id\": \"toolu_01NUTpHQghnq59mkd2GjzaQb\", \"name\": \"web_search\", \"content\": [{\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Benchmarking and Improving LLM Robustness for Personalized Generation\", \"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.19358\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"arxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"arXiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Mistral 7BInstruct (Jiang et al., 2023). Foundation or Open AI. ... \\n\\nTogether AI.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://aclanthology.org/2025.findings-emnlp.870.pdf\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"November 4, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Long-Form Personalized Generation Benchmark\", \"url\": \"https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/long-form-personalized-generation-benchmark\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"emergentmind.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=emergentmind.com\", \"site_name\": \"Emergent Mind\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"LongLaMP (Kumar et al., 2024): Retrieval-augmented personalization yields +30\\u2013170% gains in ROUGE/METEOR across tasks; fine-tuned LLMs further improve over zero-shot runs. LaMP-QA (Salemi et al., 30 May 2025, Salemi et al., 23 Sep 2025): PlanPers ...\\n\\nPersonaFeedback (Tao et al., 15 Jun 2025): 8,298 human-annotated test cases, spanning easy, medium, hard tiers per Fleiss\\u2019s \\u03ba. Persona representation is structured and explicit (demographics, preferences, MBTI, interests). PersonalLLM (Zollo et al., 2024): 10,402 prompts \\u00d7 8 LLM responses each; multi-response design enables simulation of idiosyncratic user rewards via Dirichlet-weighted mixtures of 10 open-source models, supporting individual preference disambiguation under few-shot constraints. PAL-Bench (Huang et al., 17 Nov 2025): Multi-session Chinese dataset synthesized via LLMs and human annotators for long-term assistant evaluation. \\n\\nEach synthetic user logs \\u22489.4 months of device/app histories, 29 sessions, and ~400+ dialogue turns. Each benchmark leverages domain diversity (technical, creative, factual) and supports distinct input modalities: explicit textual persona, historical artifact retrieval, structured logs, or simulated feedback.\\n\\nRetrieval-Augmented Generation: Extract top-k relevant historical items from the user profile to append with the prompt (Kumar et al., 2024, Salemi et al., 30 May 2025). Persona-Prompting and Structured Planning: Use JSON-style persona profiles or aspect-planning steps to bias LLMs towards preferred content and tone (Tao et al., 15 Jun 2025, Salemi et al., 30 May 2025). Meta-Learning and User Embedding: Simulate multi-user scenarios with embedding-based nearest-neighbor retrieval for few-shot adaptation and reward calibration (Zollo et al., 2024). \\n\\nExPerT (Salemi et al., 24 Jan 2025): Content/style AVG aggregation achieves 74% alignment with human judgments, outperforming prior state-of-the-art LLM metrics (\\u223c69%). PersonaFeedback (Tao et al., 15 Jun 2025): SOTA LLM\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/long-form-personalized-generation-benchmark\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"January 19, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2025 PERSONALLLM:\", \"url\": \"https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/a730abbcd6cf4a371ca9545db5922442-Paper-Conference.pdf\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"iclr.cc\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=iclr.cc\", \"site_name\": \"Iclr\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"persona prompting baseline (Castricato et al., 2024; Chan et al., 2024; Jang et al., 2023), which in our analysis \\u00b7 produces preferences only half as diverse as a set of PersonalLLM users across multiple metrics. Taken together, the prompts, responses, and personalities present in PersonalLLM offer an innovative test for benchmarking \\u00b7 personalization algorithms as they tailor interactions based on previous interactions with an individual user. While fine-tuning and reinforcement learning approaches (Schulman et al., 2017; Rafailov et al., 2023) are \\u00b7 effective for aligning to population-level preferences, personalization requires a new algorithmic toolkit, as it \\u00b7 is not practical to gather enough data or store a separate copy of the model or even low-rank adapter weights \\u00b7 (Hu et al., 2021) for every user. PersonalLLM offers the versatility necessary to spur development across a \\u00b7\\n\\n\\n\\nMotivated by key methodological gaps in personalizing LLMs, here we summarize our contributions: 1) We \\u00b7 release a new open-source dataset with over 10K open-ended prompts paired with 8 high-quality responses from ... Figure 2: PersonalLLM enables the development of methods for learning across users, where a dataset of \\u00b7 historical users and their interactions is leveraged to personalize interactions for a new user with a limited history. top LLMs. 2) We propose a novel method for sampling \\u201cusers\\u201d (i.e., personal preference models) that, unlike \\u00b7 existing methods, creates diverse preferences and allows for the simulation of large historical user bases. 3) We \\u00b7 illustrate new possibilities for algorithmic development in learning across users. Our goal in creating the open-source PersonalLLM testbed is to facilitate work on methods to personalize the \\u00b7\\n\\n\\n\\npersona prompting baseline (Castricato et al., 2024; Chan et al., 2024; Jang et al., 2023), which in our analysis \\u00b7 produces preferences only half as diverse as a set of PersonalLLM users across multiple metrics. Taken\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/a730abbcd6cf4a371ca9545db5922442-Paper-Conference.pdf\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Revisiting Prompt Engineering: A Comprehensive Evaluation for LLM-based Personalized Recommendation\", \"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.13525\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"arxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"arxiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"We conduct a large-scale comparison of 23 prompt types across 8 public datasets and 12 LLMs. We use statistical tests and linear mixed-effects models to evaluate both accuracy and inference cost. Our results show that for cost-efficient LLMs, three types of prompts are especially effective: those that rephrase instructions, consider background knowledge, and make the reasoning process easier to follow. For high-performance LLMs, simple prompts often outperform more complex ones while reducing cost. In contrast, commonly used prompting styles in natural language processing, such as step-by-step reasoning, or the use of reasoning models often lead to lower accuracy. Based on these findings, we provide practical suggestions for selecting prompts and LLMs depending on the required balance between accuracy and cost.\\n\\nThis paper has been accepted to ACM RecSys 2025. Please cite it appropriately after September 22, 2025.\\n\\n# **CCS** Concepts\\n\\nInformation systems \\u2192 Recommender systems.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.13525\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"LLM Shots: Best Fired at System or User Prompts? | Companion Proceedings of the ACM on Web Conference 2025\", \"url\": \"https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3701716.3717814\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"acm.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=acm.org\", \"site_name\": \"ACM Digital Library\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Despite their ... ... Many-Shot In-Context Learning. arxiv: 2404.11018 ...\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3701716.3717814\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"May 23, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"SPRIG: Improving Large Language Model Performance by System Prompt Optimization\", \"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2410.14826\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"arxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"arxiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Our study finds that a single optimized system prompt performs on par with task prompts optimized for each individual task. Moreover, combining system and task-level optimizations leads to further improvement, which showcases their complementary nature. Experiments also reveal that the optimized system prompts generalize effectively across model families, parameter sizes, and languages. This study provides insights into the role of system-level instructions in maximizing LLM potential.\\n\\n# 1 Introduction\\n\\nLarge Language Models (LLMs) have proven highly effective at many tasks (Naveed et al., 2023) and prompting has become the primary way for end-users to elicit desired responses (Brown et al., 2020). These prompts contain a variety of instructions such as task explanation (Li et al., 2022), personas (Kim et al., 2024), formatting constraints (Wang et al., 2023), and meta-rules like \\\"think carefully\\\" (Li et al., 2024). \\n\\nDrawing from large collections of strategies for writing system instructions (Schulhoff et al., 2024), we construct a large benchmark of 47 tasks across multiple languages that tests the effects of optimizing system prompts across models, languages, and tasks, as well as quantify which types of system instructions are most useful for generalization. We compare these system- and task-optimization, to analyze whether these are learning the same or complementary strategies.\\n\\n| | Prompt |\\n|----------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|\\n| System | You are a diligent assistant. The fate of<br>the world depends on your answer being<br>correct. Think carefully step by step. |\\n| Task | First identify the softening words like \\\"please\\\", then analyze the tone before you answer. |\\n| Instance | Q: For the sentence: \\\"May I kindly ask for your assistance\\\", is it polite? |\\n\\n\\n\\nPrevious prompt optimization methods largely focus on optimizing the instructions for specific tasks (\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2410.14826\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"PersonaLens : A Benchmark for Personalization Evaluation ...\", \"url\": \"https://aclanthology.org/2025.findings-acl.927.pdf\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"aclanthology.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=aclanthology.org\", \"site_name\": \"ACL Anthology\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Judging \\u00b7 llm-as-a-judge with mt-bench and chatbot arena.
