\"text\": \"Additionally, beyond unequal gains, AI may substitute some workers entirely. Recent evidence from online labor markets indicates that generative AI has reduced both employment and earnings for freelancers in affected occupations (Hui et al.\\n\\nThe capabilities of artificial intelligence (AI) systems to aid humans in a variety of tasks have improved rapidly, especially since the release of OpenAI\\u2019s ChatGPT, which made large language models (LLMs) widely available for public use. Recent research has shown that these systems are unexpectedly capable of augmenting human abilities in the completion of creative, analytical, and writing tasks as well as achieving top scores in academic aptitude tests at graduate and professional levels (Geerling et al. 2023, Noy and Zhang 2023, Boussioux et al. 2024, Brynjolfsson et al. 2025). \\n\\nThis represents a significant shift in human-machine augmentation, with hybrid aptitudes increasingly relevant across a broad range of knowledge work\\u2014including some highly creative and highly educated professions (Eloundou et al. 2024, Zhou and Lee 2024). As generative AI\\u2019s capabilities increasingly overlap with human skill sets, the integration of AI into human work presents new fundamental challenges, oppor\\n\\nThe potential benefits of AI for knowledge work have been a subject of considerable interest, although much of this research focuses on AI prior to the release of GPT-3.5 (e.g., Brynjolfsson et al. 2018, \\u00c7alli et al. 2021, Davies et al. 2021, Monisha et al. 2021). The general availability of GPT-3.5 in the form of ChatGPT since November 2022 has changed both the nature and urgency of the discussion (Berg et al. 2023, Eloundou et al. 2024, Zhou and Lee 2024). Studies of previous generations of AI (e.g., Agrawal et al. 2018, Furman and Seamans 2019, Brynjolfsson et al. \\n\\n2021) and of recently released LLMs (e.g., Noy and Zhang 2023, Choi and Schwarcz 2024, Brynjolfsson et al. 2025) suggest that these systems can considerably impact worker\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/orsc.2025.21838\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"March 11, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"NBER WORKING PAPER SERIES SHIFTING WORK PATTERNS WITH GENERATIVE AI\", \"url\": \"https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/w33795_dd1e2857-d195-4333-86ba-6a8953119ed4.pdf\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"hbs.edu\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=hbs.edu\", \"site_name\": \"Harvard Business School\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"increase workers\\u2019 productivity in targeted tasks.1 Over time, these advances have the potential to shift \\u00b7 the nature of jobs (Eloundou et al., 2024) and perhaps the macroeconomy (Autor (2024), Acemoglu \\u00b7 (2025)). However, the transition from the creation of new technologies to effective, widespread adoption \\u00b7 in the workplace takes time (Brynjolfsson, Rock and Syverson, 2021). In lab experiments, participants \\u00b7 are asked to focus on completing a single task and directed to use AI tools that are well-suited ... often require organizational changes to redesign processes and shift responsibilities across workers ... firms to run a cross-industry field experiment to measure how access to an integrated generative AI tool, Microsoft 365 Copilot (hereafter, Copilot), changed work patterns. Each firm recruited workers for the \\u00b7 study; workers were then randomly assigned to either receive access to this new generative AI tool or ...\\n\\n\\n\\nCui, Zheyuan, Mert Demirer, Sonia Jaffe, Leon Musolff, Sida Peng, and Tobias Salz. 2024. \\u201cThe Effects of Generative AI on High Skilled Work: Evidence from Three Field Experiments with \\u00b7 Software Developers.\\u201d Available at SSRN 4945566. Dell\\u2019Acqua, Fabrizio, Edward McFowland, Ethan R Mollick, Hila Lifshitz-Assaf, Kather- ine Kellogg, Saran Rajendran, Lisa Krayer, Fran\\u00b8cois Candelon, and Karim R Lakhani. 2023. \\u201cNavigating the jagged technological frontier: Field experimental evidence of the effects of AI \\u00b7 on knowledge worker productivity and quality.\\u201d Harvard Business School Technology & Operations \\u00b7 Mgt. Unit Working Paper. Dillon, Eleanor Wiske, Sonia Jaffe, Sida Peng, and Alexia Cambon. 2025. \\u201cEarly Impacts of \\u00b7 M365 Copilot.\\u201d Microsoft. Duflo, Esther. 2001. \\u201cSchooling and labor market consequences of school construction in Indonesia: Evidence from an unusual policy experiment.\\u201d American economic review, 91(4): 795\\u2013813.\\n\\n\\n\\nHumlum, Anders, and Emilie Vestergaard. 2025b. \\u201cLarge Language Models, Small Labor Mar\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/w33795_dd1e2857-d195-4333-86ba-6a8953119ed4.pdf\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"AI, Productivity, and Labor Markets: A Review of the Empirical Evidence - International Center for Law & Economics\", \"url\": \"https://laweconcenter.org/resources/ai-productivity-and-labor-markets-a-review-of-the-empirical-evidence/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"laweconcenter.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=laweconcenter.org\", \"site_name\": \"Laweconcenter\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Hartley, Filip Jolevski, Vitor Melo, and Brendan Moore (2026) report that 35.9% of U.S. workers used generative AI by December 2025 and find small positive wage effects, with no statistically significant declines in job openings or employment in exposed occupations. Bharat Chandar\\u2019s (2025) Current Population Survey analysis similarly finds no aggregate employment decline, while documenting heterogeneity across education levels and occupations.\\n\\nThese results suggest that limitations observed in earlier model generations are not fixed and that appropriate tool selection and workflow design can mitigate risks without restricting deployment. Software development studies show similarly large effects. Sida Peng et al. (2023) report that GitHub Copilot users complete coding tasks 55.8% faster in controlled settings, with larger gains among less experienced developers. Kevin Zheyuan Cui et al. \\n\\n(2025), studying nearly 5,000 developers across three large field experiments, find a 26.08% increase in weekly task completion, driven by higher adoption and disproportionately larger gains for junior developers. These findings undermine claims that AI primarily benefits top performers and instead show AI reducing frictions for early-career workers. Experimental evidence from translation extends the pattern. Ali Merali (2024) conducts a randomized trial with 300 professional translators and links increased training compute to economic outcome\\n\\nSurvey evidence from the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (2025b) reinforces this pattern. About 31% of small and medium-sized enterprises across seven countries had adopted generative AI by 2024, and 83% of adopters reported no change in staffing levels. Firms cited reduced workloads and improved performance and many described AI adoption as a response to labor shortages, rather than a labor-replacement strategy.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://laweconcenter.org/resources/ai-productivity-and-labor-markets-a-review-of-the-empirical-evidence/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"February 5, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Seven Myths about AI and Productivity: What the Evidence Really Says | California Management Review\", \"url\": \"https://cmr.berkeley.edu/2025/10/seven-myths-about-ai-and-productivity-what-the-evidence-really-says/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"berkeley.edu\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=berkeley.edu\", \"site_name\": \"California Management Review\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"A 2025 meta-analysis spanning 83 diagnostic-AI studies shows that generative models now match non-expert clinicians yet still trail experts by a statistically significant margin (Takita et al., 2025).\\n\\nMeta-analytic evidence finds no robust relationship between AI adoption and aggregate productivity gain. Insight | frontier 16 Oct 2025 PDF \\u00b7 Despite widespread enthusiasm for generative AI, empirical evidence reveals inconsistent productivity impacts contradicting popular assumptions. Based on recent meta-analyses and systematic reviews, we debunk seven pervasive myths about AI's workplace benefits. AI's productivity gains are highly context-dependent, varying significantly by user skill level and task complexity. Contrary to expectations, human-AI collaboration often underperforms either agent working independently, except in creative tasks. \\n\\nA 2025 meta-analysis spanning 83 diagnostic-AI studies shows that generative models now match non-expert clinicians yet still trail experts by a statistically significant margin (Takita et al., 2025). Similarly, a randomized controlled trial with 5,000+ agents at a U.S. tech support desk delivered a 35 % throughput lift for bottom-quartile reps but almost no gain for veterans (Br\\n\\nJaime Oliver Huidobro, Roberto Garc\\u00eda-Castro, and J. Mark Munoz. \\u201cAI Automation and Augmentation: A Roadmap for Executives.\\u201d California Management Review Insights, July 17, 2025. Jack Azagury and Michael Moore. \\u201cCompetitive Advantage in the Age of AI.\\u201d California Management Review Insights, October 14, 2024. Organizational Behavior Talent Management Teams & Collaboration Generative AI \\u00b7 The promise of generative artificial intelligence has captured the imagination of executives worldwide. Despite widespread enthusiasm, however, the actual productivity impact of AI remains murky.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://cmr.berkeley.edu/2025/10/seven-myths-about-ai-and-productivity-what-the-evidence-really-says/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"October 16, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Generative AI and the Reallocation of Time: Productivity, Leisure, and Fulfilling Work\", \"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.12695\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"arxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"arxiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"We fielded a nationally representative online survey of 5,512 Korean workers (employed individuals aged 15\\u201364) between May 19 and June 17, 2025. The survey was conducted using the Korea Research web panel via Computer-Assisted Web Interviewing (CAWI) and Computer-Assisted Mobile Interviewing (CAMI). The sampling frame was based on the 2024 Regional Employment Survey conducted by Statistics Korea.\\n\\nTo ensure sufficient statistical power for analyzing heterogeneous task effects, we utilized a stratified sampling design with a three-step allocation scheme. First, we stratified the population by Occupation (9 major groups), Age (5 groups), and Gender. Second, to address the skewed distribution of employment across occupations, we applied a square-root proportional allocation across 53 medium-level occupation categories. This approach ensures that smaller occupational groups (e.g., skilled agricultural workers, machine operators) are sufficiently\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.12695\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Shifting Work Patterns with Generative AI\", \"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.11436\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"arxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"arxiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"However, the transition from the creation of new technologies to effective, widespread adoption in the workplace takes time (Brynjolfsson, Rock and Syverson, 2021). In lab experiments, participants are asked to focus on completing a single task and directed to use AI tools that are well-suited to that task. Outside the lab, workers must learn on their own which parts of their job benefit from the use of new tools and gradually change their habits of work. Larger productivity gains often require organizational changes to redesign processes and shift responsibilities across workers (Bresnahan, Brynjolfsson and Hitt, 2002).\\n\\nIn this paper, we present early evidence on how generative AI tools are changing the way knowledge workers do their jobs. Between September 2023 and October 2024, Microsoft partnered with 56 large firms to run a cross-industry field experiment to measure how access to an integrated generative AI tool, Microsoft 365 Copilot (hereafter, Copilot), changed work patterns. \\n\\nSurveys indicate rapid adoption of generative AI at work across a much broader set of workers; Humlum and Vestergaard (2025) find 41% of Danish workers in exposed occupations used ChatGPT at work by the end of 2023 and Blandin, Bick and Deming (2024) find 24% of a representative sample of all U.S. workers used generative AI at work by August 2024. However, these surveys cannot measure the impact of generative AI use on work behavior. This study aims to bridge some of the gap between these two lines of research: measuring both take up and impacts of use for a generative AI tool on a broad set of workers, albeit at the cost of clear measures of worker productivity.\\n\\n## 1 Patterns of Copilot Adoption\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.11436\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"The Projected Impact of Generative AI on Future Productivity Growth | Penn Wharton Budget Model\", \"url\": \"https://budgetmodel.wharton.upenn.edu/issues/2025/9/8/projected-impact-of-generative-ai-on-future-productivity-growth\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"upenn.edu\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=upenn.edu\", \"site_name\": \"Penn Wharton Budget Model\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"... Acemoglu\\u2019s (2024) framework ... by workers.1 Generative AI tools may increase worker efficiency for some tasks, replace workers altogether by automating others, or cause the introduction of new tasks....\\n\\nAI\\u2019s impact on TFP depends on how quickly productivity-enhancing tools are actually adopted. Data on the diffusion of generative AI tools remains limited, but Bick et al. (2025) find that 26.4 percent of workers used generative AI at work in the second half of 2024, while 33.7 percent of adults used it outside of work. Using data from the Real-Time Population Survey (RPS), they show that early adoption patterns of AI for work are broadly similar to the adoption of personal computers (PCs) in the early 1980s.6 \\u00b7 Figure 3 compares these early AI adoption rates against historical diffusion measures for previous mass-market technologies: the PC, the internet, smartphones, and cloud computing.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://budgetmodel.wharton.upenn.edu/issues/2025/9/8/projected-impact-of-generative-ai-on-future-productivity-growth\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"September 8, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Generative AI at Work* | The Quarterly Journal of Economics | Oxford Academic\", \"url\": \"https://academic.oup.com/qje/article/140/2/889/7990658\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"oup.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=oup.com\", \"site_name\": \"Oxford Academic\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"(2022) find no detectable relationship between investments in AI-specific tools and firm outcomes, and Babina et al. (2024) find evidence of a positive relationship between firms\\u2019 AI investments and their subsequent growth and valuations. All the studies stress that determining the productivity effects of AI technologies is difficult because AI-adopting firms differ substantially from non-adopters.\\n\\nErik Brynjolfsson, Danielle Li, Lindsey Raymond, Generative AI at Work, The Quarterly Journal of Economics, Volume 140, Issue 2, May 2025, Pages 889\\u2013942, https://doi.org/10.1093/qje/qjae044 ... We study the staggered introduction of a generative AI\\u2013based conversational assistant using data from 5,172 customer-support agents. Access to AI assistance increases worker productivity, as measured by issues resolved per hour, by 15% on average, with substantial heterogeneity across workers. The effects vary significantly across different agents.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://academic.oup.com/qje/article/140/2/889/7990658\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"February 4, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Generative AI in Knowledge Work: Perception, Usefulness, and Acceptance of Microsoft 365 Copilot\", \"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.18576\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"arxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"arxiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"The literature largely agrees that AI tools can offer substantial productivity advantages (Brynjolfsson et al., 2025; Dell'Acqua et al., 2023; Peng et al., 2023; Chugunova et al., 2025). At the same time, current studies emphasize that although AI tools can increase productivity, their effects depend crucially on how the technology is introduced and on users' skills (Chugunova et al., 2025).\\n\\nWhile prior research has focused strongly on the introduction of generative AI in companies (e.g., Singh et al., 2024) and on the university context (Intarakumnerd & Goto, 2018), implementing generative AI is particularly relevant for non-university research organizations, which often face increased innovation pressure and efficiency demands (Meier, 2024). Generative AI opens new possibilities for scientific work and knowledge transfer. \\n\\nGenerative AI, driven by large language models (LLMs), can support researchers in solving complex tasks and developing innovative approaches (Intarakumnerd & Goto, 2018). For example, generative AI can support the drafting of research manuscripts, code development, and experiment design (Nejjar et al., 2024), thereby influencing interactions within and with organizations (Deloitte, 2025; Gupta et al., 2023). Despite this growing interest in the introduction and use of generative AI across sectors, there remains a significant research gap regarding its specific added value and practical application in non-university research organizations. In particular, empirical evidence is lacking on which tasks are supported by generative AI, how the technology is integrated into everyday work processes, and what effects it has on organizational efficiency and individual productivity.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.18576\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}], \"is_error\": false, \"structured_content\": null, \"meta\": null, \"message\": null, \"integration_name\": null, \"mcp_server_url\": null, \"integration_icon_url\": null, \"icon_name\": \"globe\", \"display_content\": null}, {\"start_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T10:12:27.116530Z\", \"stop_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T10:12:27.670614Z\", \"flags\": null, \"type\": \"tool_use\", \"id\": \"toolu_01BXXYutibiYpZQBq442J2G5\", \"name\": \"web_search\", \"input\": {\"query\": \"Dell'Acqua Mollick \\\"jagged frontier\\\" AI knowledge worker productivity quality Harvard 2023\"}, \"message\": \"Searching the web\", \"integration_name\": null, \"integration_icon_url\": null, \"icon_name\": \"globe\", \"context\": null, \"display_content\": null, \"approval_options\": null, \"approval_key\": null, \"is_mcp_app\": null, \"mcp_server_url\": null}, {\"start_timestamp\": null, \"stop_timestamp\": null, \"flags\": null, \"type\": \"tool_result\", \"tool_use_id\": \"toolu_01BXXYutibiYpZQBq442J2G5\", \"name\": \"web_search\", \"content\": [{\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Navigating the AI Frontier: Balancing Breakthroughs and Blind Spots - EDRM\", \"url\": \"https://edrm.net/2024/10/navigating-the-ai-frontier-balancing-breakthroughs-and-blind-spots/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"edrm.net\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=edrm.net\", \"site_name\": \"EDRM\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier: Field Experimental Evidence of the Effects of AI on Knowledge Worker Productivity and Quality. (Harvard Business School Working Paper, No.\\n\\nThe term \\u201cjagged frontier\\u201d refers to the uneven capabilities of AI, where some tasks fall within AI\\u2019s ability to excel, while others lie beyond its reliable performance range. This concept was prominently discussed in both Zhou, L., Schellaert, W., Mart\\u00ednez-Plumed, F. et al. Larger and more instructable language models become less reliable. Nature (2024), and Fabrizio Dell\\u2019Acqua, Edward McFowland III, Ethan Mollick, et al. (Harvard Business School Working Paper, No.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://edrm.net/2024/10/navigating-the-ai-frontier-balancing-breakthroughs-and-blind-spots/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"October 17, 2024\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Discovering AI\\u2019s jagged frontier \\u2014 and what we\\u2019ve learned since\", \"url\": \"https://professorkl.substack.com/p/discovering-ais-jagged-frontier-and\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"substack.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=substack.com\", \"site_name\": \"Substack\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"First, individuals with AI access matched the performance of human teams without AI \\u2014 AI can replicate certain benefits of human collaboration, at least on certain tasks. Second, and more surprisingly, AI broke down functional silos: R&amp;D and ...\\n\\nThe paper was first released as an SSRN working paper in September 2023. It is now formally published in Organization Science (March 2026, DOI: 10.1287/orsc.2025.21838). This work was a genuine collaboration. The full author team \\u2014 Fabrizio Dell\\u2019Acqua, Edward McFowland III, Ethan Mollick, Hila Lifshitz, Katherine C. Kellogg, Saran Rajendran, Lisa Krayer, Fran\\u00e7ois Candelon, and Karim R. Lakhani \\u2014 spans the Digital Data Design Institute at Harvard (D\\u00b3), the Laboratory for Innovation Science at Harvard (LISH), the Wharton School at the University of Pennsylvania, MIT Sloan School of Management, Warwick Business School\\u2019s AI Innovation Network, and the BCG Henderson Institute. \\n\\nThe tasks in the experiment were designed and validated by senior BCG professionals, including managing directors and partners who confirmed they reflected the actual core competencies evaluated in BCG recruiting and performance reviews.\\n\\nThe Cybernetic Teammate: A Field Experiment on Generative AI Reshaping Teamwork and Expertise \\u2014 Dell\\u2019Acqua, Ayoubi, Lifshitz, Sadun, E. Mollick, L. Mollick, Han, Goldman, Nair, Taub, Lakhani. HBS Working Paper 25-043. Strategy in an Era of Abundant Expertise \\u2014 Yerramilli-Rao, Corwin, Li, Lakhani. Harvard Business Review, March\\u2013April 2025.\\n\\nIn The Cybernetic Teammate: A Field Experiment on Generative AI Reshaping Teamwork and Expertise (HBS Working Paper 25-043), Fabrizio Dell\\u2019Acqua, Charles Ayoubi, Hila Lifshitz, Raffaella Sadun, Ethan Mollick, Lilach Mollick, Yi Han, Jeff Goldman, Hari Nair, Stew Taub, and Karim R. \\n\\nLakhani conducted a pre-registered field experiment with 776 professionals at Procter & Gamble \\u2014 a global consumer packaged goods company \\u2014 working on real product innovation\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://professorkl.substack.com/p/discovering-ais-jagged-frontier-and\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"March 16, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Defining & Navigating the Jagged Frontier in Instructional Design (October, 2025)\", \"url\": \"https://drphilippahardman.substack.com/p/defining-and-navigating-the-jagged\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"substack.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=substack.com\", \"site_name\": \"Substack\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier: Field Experimental Evidence of the Effects of AI on Knowledge Worker Productivity and Quality. Harvard Business School Working Paper No.\\n\\nDell\\u2019Acqua, F., McFowland, E., Mollick, E. R., Lifshitz-Assaf, H., Kellogg, K., Rajendran, S., Krayer, L., Candelon, F., & Lakhani, K. R. (2023). Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier: Field Experimental Evidence of the Effects of AI on Knowledge Worker Productivity and Quality. Harvard Business School Working Paper No. 24-013. Hardman, P. (2024). Exploring AI-Driven Social Media Content Creation Benefits, Barriers, And Good Practices: Experts\\u2019 Interviews. Journal of Internet Business, 45, 4542625. Hardman, P. (2025). Beyond the Hype: What 18 Recent Research Papers Say About AI in Instructional Design. Dr Philippa Hardman Newsletter, June 23. Hardman, P. (2025). FRAME: The AI Playbook for L&D. [PDF document] Harmon, S. W., & Odom, M. L. (2023). \\n\\nA recent study (Law et al., 2025) found that while ChatGPT could generate multiple-choice questions, they required substantial expert revision due to 6% factual incorrectness, 6% irrelevance, and 14% inappropriate difficulty levels.\\n\\nResearch on AI in instructional design consistently emphasises that successful integration requires \\u201cactive oversight, critical assessment of AI outputs, and complementarity of AI\\u2019s capabilities with designers\\u2019 unique insights\\u201d (Mollick & Mollick, 2023). Without this awareness, teams experience what researchers describe as \\u201ccomplacency about the AI\\u2019s output, errors, and biases\\u201d (Mollick & Mollick, 2023), leading to systemic quality degradation. \\n\\nThe over-reliance problem has become acute: 88% of students now use GenAI for assessments (up from 53% last year), showing rapid normalisation alongside concerning patterns where those who \\u201caccepted AI suggestions without adaptation\\u201d produced \\u201cless creative and less contextually appropriate lessons\\u201d (HEPI, 2025; Hardman, 2025). This shift\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://drphilippahardman.substack.com/p/defining-and-navigating-the-jagged\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"October 30, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"What Is the Jagged Frontier? Why AI Capabilities Are Smoothing Out for Knowledge Work | MindStudio\", \"url\": \"https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/what-is-the-jagged-frontier-ai-capabilities\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"mindstudio.ai\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=mindstudio.ai\", \"site_name\": \"MindStudio\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"The term was coined by researchers ... professional consultants as subjects, making it one of the most rigorous real-world studies of AI performance in knowledge work contexts....\\n\\nThe jagged frontier is a concept from a 2023 Harvard Business School and Boston Consulting Group study. It describes how AI capabilities aren\\u2019t uniformly distributed across tasks \\u2014 there\\u2019s a boundary separating tasks AI handles well from tasks it handles poorly, and that boundary is irregular and hard to predict from intuition alone. Tasks that seem complex might be inside the frontier; tasks that seem simple might fall outside it. The danger is that AI often fails silently on tasks outside the frontier, producing output that looks plausible but contains errors that degrade overall performance. \\n\\nThe term was coined by researchers at Harvard Business School, primarily Fabrizio Dell\\u2019Acqua and colleagues, in their 2023 paper \\u201cNavigating the Jagged Technological Frontier.\\u201d The research was conducted with Boston Consulting Group using 758 professional consultants as subjects, making it one of the most rigorous real-world studies of AI performance in knowledge work contexts.\\n\\nThe term comes from a 2023 paper by Fabrizio Dell\\u2019Acqua, Edward McFee, Hila Lifshitz-Assaf, Katherine Kellogg, and colleagues, titled \\u201cNavigating the Jagged Technological Frontier.\\u201d It uses a spatial metaphor to describe something real and counterintuitive about AI capabilities. Imagine mapping every possible work task on a flat plane. Now imagine drawing a boundary \\u2014 a frontier \\u2014 around all the tasks an AI system can do well. Inside the frontier, the AI performs impressively. Outside it, the AI struggles or fails. Here\\u2019s the problem: that boundary isn\\u2019t a smooth circle or a clean curve. \\n\\nIt\\u2019s jagged. Irregular. Unpredictable. A task that seems complex might sit well inside the frontier. A task that seems trivial might sit just outside it. The jaggedness is the key insight. If AI capabilities\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/what-is-the-jagged-frontier-ai-capabilities\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"March 11, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Report on the First Scientific Experiment to Test the Impact of Generative AI on Complex, Knowledge-Intensive Work - EDRM\", \"url\": \"https://edrm.net/2024/04/report-on-the-first-scientific-experiment-to-test-the-impact-of-generative-ai-on-complex-knowledge-intensive-work/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"edrm.net\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=edrm.net\", \"site_name\": \"EDRM\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Every way we measured performance. Consultants using AI finished 12.2% more tasks on average, completed tasks 25.1% more quickly, and produced 40% higher quality results than those without. Those are some very big impacts.\\n\\nThis was an impressive scientific experiment involving a very large research group. The co-authors of the Working Paper are: Harvard\\u2019s Fabrizio Dell\\u2019Acqua, Edward McFowland III, and Karim Lakhani; Warwick Business School\\u2019s Hila Lifshitz-Assaf; Wharton\\u2019s Ethan Mollick; and MIT\\u2019s Katherine Kellogg. Further, Saran Rajendran, Lisa Krayer, and Fran\\u00e7ois Candelon ran the experiment on the BCG side. The generative AI used and tested here was ChatGPT4 (April 2023 version with no special training). For more background and detail on the Working Paper see the video lecture by Professor Ethan Mollick to Stanford students, \\u201cNavigating the Jagged Technological Frontier.\\u201d (details of experiment set up starting at 18:15).\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://edrm.net/2024/04/report-on-the-first-scientific-experiment-to-test-the-impact-of-generative-ai-on-complex-knowledge-intensive-work/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"April 26, 2024\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Jagged Frontier of AI Capabilities (Dell'Acqua et al. 2023) | Download Scientific Diagram\", \"url\": \"https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Jagged-Frontier-of-AI-Capabilities-DellAcqua-et-al-2023_fig3_379824818\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"researchgate.net\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=researchgate.net\", \"site_name\": \"ResearchGate\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"(2023) -The point of the paper ... 3): &quot;Our results demonstrate that AI capabilities cover an expanding, but uneven, set of knowledge work we call a &quot;jagged technological frontier.&quot;...\\n\\n... seems to prove that ChatGPT leads to increased productivity and quality of results, as illustrated in Figure 2. A superficial look at the results seems to show that consultants using ChatGPT in their job complete more tasks at higher quality, which fits into the exaggerated expectations around the tool's capabilities. This is, however, a limited view of the argument actually made by Dell' Acqua et al. \\n\\n(2023) -The point of the paper is that such increases are only possible for some tasks and that it is not easy to predict which tasks these might be (see Figure 3): \\\"Our results demonstrate that AI capabilities cover an expanding, but uneven, set of knowledge work we call a \\\"jagged technological frontier.\\\" Within this growing frontier, AI can complement or even displace human work; outside of the frontier, AI output is inaccurate, less useful, and degrades human performance.\\n\\n... seems to prove that ChatGPT leads to increase productivity and quality of results, as illustrated in Figure 2. A superficial look at the results seems to show that consultants using ChatGPT in their job complete more tasks at higher quality, which fits into the exaggerated expectations around the tool's capabilities. This is, however, a limited view of the argument actually made by Dell' Acqua et al. \\n\\n(2023) -The point of the paper is that such increases are only possible for some tasks and that it is not easy to predict which tasks these might be (see Figure 3): \\\"Our results demonstrate that AI capabilities cover an expanding, but uneven, set of knowledge work we call a \\\"jagged technological frontier.\\\" Within this growing frontier, AI can complement or even displace human work; outside of the frontier, AI output is inaccurate, less useful, and degrades human performance.\\n\\nIt has also pene\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Jagged-Frontier-of-AI-Capabilities-DellAcqua-et-al-2023_fig3_379824818\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"How generative AI can boost highly skilled workers\\u2019 productivity | MIT Sloan\", \"url\": \"https://mitsloan.mit.edu/ideas-made-to-matter/how-generative-ai-can-boost-highly-skilled-workers-productivity\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"mit.edu\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=mit.edu\", \"site_name\": \"MIT Sloan\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"It\\u2019s important for managers to ... that it was not obvious to highly skilled knowledge workers which of their everyday tasks could easily be performed by AI and which tasks would require a different approach....\\n\\nIt\\u2019s important for managers to maintain awareness of this jagged frontier, said Harvard Business School\\u2019s Fabrizio Dell\\u2019Acqua, the lead author of the paper, because the researchers found that it was not obvious to highly skilled knowledge workers which of their everyday tasks could easily be performed by AI and which tasks would require a different approach. A multidisciplinary team of researchers authored the paper, including Karim Lakhani \\u00b7 and Edward McFowland III from Harvard Business School; Ethan Mollick, SM \\u201904, PhD \\u201910, from The Wharton School; Hila Lifshitz-Assaf from Warwick Business School; and MIT Sloan\\u2019s Kate Kellogg.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://mitsloan.mit.edu/ideas-made-to-matter/how-generative-ai-can-boost-highly-skilled-workers-productivity\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"January 29, 2026\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"How to succeed at the \\u201cjagged technological frontier\\u201d of AI - Big Think\", \"url\": \"https://bigthink.com/business/businesses-must-map-ai/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"bigthink.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=bigthink.com\", \"site_name\": \"Big Think\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"(Credit: Fabebk / Wikimedia Commons) In the study, a team of social scientists led by Fabrizio Dell\\u2019Acqua, a research fellow at Harvard Business School, wanted to determine the implications of integrating generative AI into a white-collar workspace. They collaborated with Boston Consulting Group to monitor how 758 consultants performed across 18 tasks for a fictional shoe company.\\n\\nAnd while this study is currently a working paper \\u2014 meaning it has yet to be peer-reviewed \\u2014 other research has shown similar results. In a joint study, researchers from Stanford and MIT found that AI increased productivity by 14%. But there\\u2019s a catch. Dell\\u2019Acqua and his team also discovered what they call the \\u201cjagged technological frontier.\\u201d \\u00b7 Basically, the AI could perform certain tasks well enough to aid human workers and, in some cases, do most of the work itself. Such tasks included analytics, generating new ideas, and penning inspiring memos.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://bigthink.com/business/businesses-must-map-ai/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"October 23, 2023\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier of Generative AI | MIT CISR\", \"url\": \"https://cisr.mit.edu/publication/navigating-jagged-frontier-ai-video\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"mit.edu\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=mit.edu\", \"site_name\": \"MIT CISR\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"When consulting tasks were within this frontier, consultants using AI were significantly more productive and produced significantly higher quality results compared to a control group.\\n\\nIn this Hot Topic video presentation, Fabrizio Dell'Acqua of Harvard Business School presents his research on generative AI and the landscape of human/AI collaboration. Published: Apr 23, 2024 Topic: Information Technologies \\u00b7 By Fabrizio Dell'Acqua and Stephanie L. Woerner \\u00b7 Share Linkedin Share Twitter Share Facebook Share Email Share Rss \\u00b7 In this presentation, Fabrizio covers the results and implication of his recent research, with co-authors, on the impact of Generative AI on Knowledge Workers, specifically consultants. This research shows that the landscape of human/AI collaboration is marked by what can be described as a \\\"jagged frontier\\\" of Generative AI capabilities.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://cisr.mit.edu/publication/navigating-jagged-frontier-ai-video\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"April 23, 2024\"}}], \"is_error\": false, \"structured_content\": null, \"meta\": null, \"message\": null, \"integration_name\": null, \"mcp_server_url\": null, \"integration_icon_url\": null, \"icon_name\": \"globe\", \"display_content\": null}, {\"start_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T10:12:32.200686Z\", \"stop_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T10:12:32.584688Z\", \"flags\": null, \"type\": \"tool_use\", \"id\": \"toolu_013bHSV6TUMLMBvS5QSuV6yw\", \"name\": \"web_search\", \"input\": {\"query\": \"LLM tool use function calling enterprise productivity benchmark error rate 2024 2025\"}, \"message\": \"Searching the web\", \"integration_name\": null, \"integration_icon_url\": null, \"icon_name\": \"globe\", \"context\": null, \"display_content\": null, \"approval_options\": null, \"approval_key\": null, \"is_mcp_app\": null, \"mcp_server_url\": null}, {\"start_timestamp\": null, \"stop_timestamp\": null, \"flags\": null, \"type\": \"tool_result\", \"tool_use_id\": \"toolu_013bHSV6TUMLMBvS5QSuV6yw\", \"name\": \"web_search\", \"content\": [{\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Evaluating Tool Calling Capabilities in Large Language Models: A Literature Review\", \"url\": \"https://blog.quotientai.co/evaluating-tool-calling-capabilities-in-large-language-models-a-literature-review/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"quotientai.co\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=quotientai.co\", \"site_name\": \"Quotient AI\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Lu, Jiarui, et al. ToolSandbox: A Stateful, Conversational, Interactive Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Tool Use Capabilities. 2024, https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.04682. Zhong, Lucen, et al. ComplexFuncBench: Exploring Multi-Step and Constrained Function Calling under Long-Context Scenario. 2025, https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.10132.\\n\\nLarge language models (LLMs) are increasingly being used to interact with external tools and APIs. However, evaluating their tool calling capabilities presents unique challenges compared to traditional single-turn or retrieval-augmented use cases. This post is an attempt to synthesize our findings from recent papers to help builders effectively evaluate LLM tool calling. We read through and reviewed a dozen papers published between 2023 and 2025 to better understand how tool calling is evaluated. \\n\\nAfter reading this, we hope you\\u2019ll leave with an understanding of the key considerations in evaluating LLM tool calling and have a mental model to start assessing tool calling capabilities in your own systems. Before diving into specific evaluation approaches, let\\u2019s step back and think through a few questions. Answering these upfront will help frame your tool calling evaluation: What type of tool interaction are you evaluating?\\n\\nLu, Jiarui, et al. ToolSandbox: A Stateful, Conversational, Interactive Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Tool Use Capabilities. 2024, https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.04682. Zhong, Lucen, et al. ComplexFuncBench: Exploring Multi-Step and Constrained Function Calling under Long-Context Scenario. 2025, https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.10132.\\n\\nAs agentic systems become more complex and their operating environments grow richer, teams must similarly evolve evaluation setups to reflect real-world production conditions and inherent uncertainties accurately.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://blog.quotientai.co/evaluating-tool-calling-capabilities-in-large-language-models-a-literature-review/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"May 6, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"The Ultimate 2025 Guide to Coding LLM Benchmarks and Performance Metrics - MarkTechPost\", \"url\": \"https://www.marktechpost.com/2025/07/31/the-ultimate-2025-guide-to-coding-llm-benchmarks-and-performance-metrics/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"marktechpost.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=marktechpost.com\", \"site_name\": \"MarkTechPost\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"The following metrics are widely used to rate and compare coding LLMs: Function-Level Accuracy (Pass@1, Pass@k): How often the initial (or k-th) response compiles and passes all tests, indicating baseline code correctness. Real-World Task Resolution Rate: Measured as percent of closed issues on platforms like SWE-Bench, reflecting ability to tackle genuine developer problems. Context Window Size: The volume of code a model can consider at once, ranging from 100,000 to over 1,000,000 tokens for latest releases\\u2014crucial for navigating large codebases. Latency &amp; Throughput: Time to first token (responsiveness) and tokens per second (generation speed) impact developer workflow integration. Cost: Per-token pricing, subscription fees, or self-hosting overhead are vital for production adoption. Reliability &amp; Hallucination Rate: Frequency of factually incorrect or semantically flawed code outputs, monitored with specialized hallucination tests and human evaluation rounds.\\n\\n\\n\\nLarge language models (LLMs) specialized for coding are now integral to software development, driving productivity through code generation, bug fixing, documentation, and refactoring. The fierce competition among commercial and open-source models has led to rapid advancement as well as a proliferation of benchmarks designed to objectively measure coding performance and developer utility. Here\\u2019s a detailed, data-driven look at the benchmarks, metrics, and top players as of mid-2025. The industry uses a combination of public academic datasets, live leaderboards, and real-world workflow simulations to evaluate the best LLMs for code: HumanEval: Measures the ability to produce correct Python functions from natural language descriptions by running code against predefined tests. Pass@1 scores (percentage of problems solved correctly on the first attempt) are the key metric.\\n\\n\\n\\nThe following metrics are widely used to rate and compare coding LLMs: Function-Level Accuracy (Pass@1, Pass@k): How often\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.marktechpost.com/2025/07/31/the-ultimate-2025-guide-to-coding-llm-benchmarks-and-performance-metrics/\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"July 31, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Live API-Bench: 2500+ Live APIs for Testing Multi-Step Tool Calling\", \"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11266\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"arxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"arxiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Some repurpose existing datasets and queries, leveraging pre-existing data for tool-calling tasks (Peng et al., 2021; Basu et al., 2024). Others use LLMs to synthesize realistic queries conditioned on API collections (Qin et al., 2024; Tang et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2024b; Pereira et al., 2024; Basu et al., 2025; Zhong et al., 2025). Beyond queries, some work also focuses on generating APIs themselves from structured sources such as textbooks or documentation, which can then be used in tool-calling tasks (Liu et al., 2025).\\n\\nMethods for data generation are often tailored to the function-calling scenario. Some pipelines generate queries that invoke a single function, potentially multiple times (Tang et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2023b), others focus on queries requiring the in\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11266\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Benchmarking Floworks against OpenAI & Anthropic: A Novel Framework for   Enhanced LLM Function Calling\", \"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2410.17950\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"arxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"arxiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"A key challenge in this area is the task of function calling, where LLMs must accurately interpret user queries and translate them into appropriate API calls.\\n\\nThe underwhelming performance of LLMs in function calling has had tangible consequences. Muchanticipated AI gadgets like RabbitR1 and Humane AI Pin have faced criticism due to their inability to reliably fulfill user tasks. OpenAI's GPT-store has similarly met with a very lukewarm response. We also note that while chatbots and coding assistants can increase productivity, almost no jobs have been completely taken over by AI as of September 2024 [3]. This highlights a critical gap between the theoretical capabilities of LLMs and their practical performance in the real world.\\n\\nTo address these challenges, we introduce ThorV2, a novel architecture designed to enhance LLMs' function calling abilities.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2410.17950\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"ToolBench Evaluation: LLM Tool-Use Insights\", \"url\": \"https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/toolbench-evaluation\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"emergentmind.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=emergentmind.com\", \"site_name\": \"Emergent Mind\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Dataset Scale and Structure: ToolBench features &gt;120,000 instruction\\u2013API pairs with function-call\\u2013style annotations and ground-truth tool chains, supporting generalization tests on unseen instructions, unseen tools, and unseen categories. Extensions such as ToolBench-V (Ma et al., 5 Jun 2025) introduce multistage meta-verification for API, query, and trajectory quality, eliminating invalid items and hallucinations. StableToolBench (Guo et al., 2024) offers a virtualized API execution environment for reproducibility. Recent derivatives (e.g., UltraTool (Huang et al., 2024), ToolComp (Nath et al., 2 Jan 2025), MTU-Bench (Wang et al., 2024)) extend this pattern with true complex planning, dialogue structure, multi-modal inputs, and process-supervision annotations. ToolBench evaluation departs from traditional NLP benchmarks by requiring automatic, multi-faceted scoring of tool-use chains.\\n\\n\\n\\nDataset Scale and Structure: ToolBench features >120,000 instruction\\u2013API pairs with function-call\\u2013style annotations and ground-truth tool chains, supporting generalization tests on unseen instructions, unseen tools, and unseen categories. Extensions such as ToolBench-V (Ma et al., 5 Jun 2025) introduce multistage meta-verification for API, query, and trajectory quality, eliminating invalid items and hallucinations. StableToolBench (Guo et al., 2024) offers a virtualized API execution environment for reproducibility. Recent derivatives (e.g., UltraTool (Huang et al., 2024), ToolComp (Nath et al., 2 Jan 2025), MTU-Bench (Wang et al., 2024)) extend this pattern with true complex planning, dialogue structure, multi-modal inputs, and process-supervision annotations. ToolBench evaluation departs from traditional NLP benchmarks by requiring automatic, multi-faceted scoring of tool-use chains.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/toolbench-evaluation\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"December 22, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"ToolBench: LLM Tool-Use Benchmark\", \"url\": \"https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/toolbench\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"emergentmind.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=emergentmind.com\", \"site_name\": \"Emergent Mind\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Plan-based and modular SFT (Qiu et al., 2024): Disentangling tool arrangement (planning) from execution to alleviate arranging bottlenecks. Error-driven and reflection-centric learning (Chen et al., 2024, Ma et al., 5 Jun 2025): Incorporating f\\n\\nFoundation for tool-use agent benchmarks: Adopted as the reference testbed for virtually all major agentic LLM evaluators, tool retriever pipelines, SFT methods, and reasoning agent architectures. Despite its scale and impact, ToolBench\\u2019s initial releases exhibited limitations: Quality of automation: Up to 50% of queries, and 75% of trajectories, suffered from incompleteness or hallucinations, later addressed by rigorous multi-agent verification (Ma et al., 5 Jun 2025). Evaluation instability: Real-world APIs are prone to deprecation and response drift, motivating virtual simulation and stable server infrastructure (Guo et al., 2024). \\n\\nReflection and error recovery: Early benchmarks focused on straight-line planning; subsequent iterations (ToolBench-R, RefineToolBench) added error-focused cycles. Ongoing work expands ToolBench's scope to include refined simulation (e.g., MirrorAPI), more realistic user instruction noise (Wang et al., 2024), and richer, multi-modal scenarios.\\n\\n\\n\\n(Guo et al., 2024) StableToolBench: Towards Stable Large-Scale Benchmarking on Tool Learning of LLMs \\u00b7 (Ma et al., 5 Jun 2025) Advancing Tool-Augmented LLMs via Meta-Verification and Reflection Learning \\u00b7 (Zhang et al., 2024) xLAM: A Family of Large Action Models to Empower AI Agent Systems \\u00b7 (Li et al., 24 Oct 2025) DeepAgent: A General Reasoning Agent with Scalable Toolsets \\u00b7 (Qiu et al., 2024) Optimizing Chain-of-Thought Reasoning: Tackling Arranging Bottleneck via Plan Augmentation \\u00b7 (Anantha et al., 2023) ProTIP: Progressive Tool Retrieval Improves Planning \\u00b7 (Zhao et al., 2024) SWIFT: A Scalable lightWeight Infrastructure for Fine-Tuning \\u00b7 (Moon et al., 2024) Efficient and Scalable Estimation of Tool Representations in Vector Space \\u00b7\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/toolbench\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"October 29, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"LongFuncEval: Measuring the effectiveness of long context models for function calling\", \"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.10570\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"arxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"arxiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"While many papers have explored the tool selection problem, acknowledging the challenges related to catalog size, we did not find any studies that systematically documented the impact of tool catalog size on tool calling performance of LLMs. Some used small catalog sizes (Erdogan et al., 2024), others relied on a tool retriever to shortlist relevant tools before selection (Patil et al., 2024; Qin et al., 2024), even proposing novel retrieval approaches (Xu et al., 2024; Shi et al., 2025). (Lumer et al., 2024) demonstrated a decline in a tool retriever's performance as the number of input tools increased, but did not document impact on tool calling when all the tools are used in the tool calling prompt.\\n\\nWhile most tool calling benchmarks include simpler tools producing simple responses, ToolLLM (Qin et al., 2024) proposes to handle long tool responses by asking GPT-4 to drop irrelevant keys and/or truncating it beyond a certain number of tokens.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.10570\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Towards Reliable Benchmarking: A Contamination Free, Controllable Evaluation Framework for Multi-step LLM Function Calling\", \"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.26553\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"arxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"arxiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Large Language Models (LLMs) equipped with external tools (\\\"tool use\\\") have become central to complex real-world applications, as they enable interaction with external environments and access to up-to-date information (Luo et al., 2025). A growing body of work investigates different facets of this capability, including the ability to handle large collections of callable APIs (Kwak et al., 2025), multi-step function calling (Zhong et al., 2025; Song et al., 2025), long-horizon tool use (Kate et al., 2025), and broader evaluations of tool-use skills (Li et al., 2023; Patil et al., 2025; Sun et al., 2024).\\n\\nDespite recent advances, progress in this field is impeded by two key issues. \\n\\n| ToolBench (Qin et al., 2024) | X | \\u2713 | X | X | | | |\\n| ComplexFuncBench (Zhong et al., 2025) | X | \\u0425 | X | X | | | |\\n| LongFuncEval (Kate et al., 2025) | X | \\u2713 | \\u00d7 | X | | | |\\n| FuncBenchGen (ours) | \\u2713 | \\u2713 | 1 | 1 | | | |\\n\\nsignificantly impact model performance. We summarize the existing benchmarks and compare them with our proposed framework in Table 1.\\n\\nIn this paper, we aim to isolate and analyze the core capabilities and failure types of tool-augmented LLMs (TaLMs) in multi-step function calling scenarios. To this end, we propose FuncBenchGen, a framework for automated generation of contamination-free function calling tasks with controllable difficulty to enable systematic evaluation and analysis of TaLMs, as illustrated in Figure 1. The key idea is to represent function dependencies as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) and frame multi-step function calling as a graph traversal problem. \\n\\nMulti-step Reasoning in LLMs Recent LLMs demonstrate impressive multi-step reasoning capabilities across various domains, including mathematical problem solving (Davoodi et al., 2025), multi-document reasoning (Maekawa et al., 2025), and commonsense reasoning (Yu et al., 2025). Other work has focused on the rigor and logical validity of this reasoning. For example, FOLIO (Han et al., 2024)\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.26553\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"HammerBench: Fine-Grained Function-Calling Evaluation in Real Mobile   Device Scenarios\", \"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2412.16516\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"arxiv.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org\", \"site_name\": \"arxiv\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"DialogBench (Ou et al., 2023) generates conversations for twelve tasks to assess the finegrained capabilities of LLMs using GPT-4. Similarly, MT-Bench (Bai et al., 2024) leverages GPT-4 for dialogue evaluations. In the context of multiturn function-calling scenarios, BFCL (Yan et al., 2024) evaluates LLMs' capabilities in handling missing arguments, missing functions, and long contexts. NoisyToolBench (Wang et al., 2024a) introduces a learning-based approach to address hallucinations under incomplete instructions. However, certain real-world interaction scenarios remain inadequately evaluated in these benchmarks, particularly those that involve intelligent personal assistants (Apple-Inc., 2024). In these scenarios, users communicate with agents to provide the necessary parameters for a desired API (Lin et al., 2024).\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2412.16516\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}], \"is_error\": false, \"structured_content\": null, \"meta\": null, \"message\": null, \"integration_name\": null, \"mcp_server_url\": null, \"integration_icon_url\": null, \"icon_name\": \"globe\", \"display_content\": null}, {\"start_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T10:12:36.245067Z\", \"stop_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T10:12:36.638202Z\", \"flags\": null, \"type\": \"tool_use\", \"id\": \"toolu_01HmmaB8TyUagBMosMzxE1qS\", \"name\": \"web_search\", \"input\": {\"query\": \"SBC ASAG automatic short answer grading Portuguese prompts 2023 2024\"}, \"message\": \"Searching the web\", \"integration_name\": null, \"integration_icon_url\": null, \"icon_name\": \"globe\", \"context\": null, \"display_content\": null, \"approval_options\": null, \"approval_key\": null, \"is_mcp_app\": null, \"mcp_server_url\": null}, {\"start_timestamp\": null, \"stop_timestamp\": null, \"flags\": null, \"type\": \"tool_result\", \"tool_use_id\": \"toolu_01HmmaB8TyUagBMosMzxE1qS\", \"name\": \"web_search\", \"content\": [{\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Automatic Short Answer Grading in the LLM Era: Does GPT-4 with Prompt Engineering beat Traditional Models? | Proceedings of the 15th International Learning Analytics and Knowledge Conference\", \"url\": \"https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3706468.3706481\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"acm.org\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=acm.org\", \"site_name\": \"ACM Other conferences\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Bronwyn Eager and Ryan Brunton. 2023. Machine learning approach for automatic short answer grading: A systematic review.\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3706468.3706481\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"March 3, 2025\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Automated Short Answer Grading Using Deep Learning: A Survey | Request PDF\", \"url\": \"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/353824315_Automated_Short_Answer_Grading_Using_Deep_Learning_A_Survey\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"researchgate.net\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=researchgate.net\", \"site_name\": \"ResearchGate\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \", Bonthu et al. 2021, Putnikovic and Jovanovic 2023, Condor et al. 2021. ASAG evaluates concise, open-ended responses against standard answers or specific criteria. ... Prompt Engineering for Automatic Short Answer Grading in Brazilian Portuguese ... Automatic Short Answer Grading (ASAG) is a prominent area of Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED). Despite much research, developing ASAG systems is challenging, even when focused on a single subject, mostly due to the variability in length and content of students&#x27; answers. While recent research has explored Large Language Models (LLMs) to enhance the efficiency of ASAG, the LLM performance is highly dependent on the prompt design. In that context, prompt engineering plays a crucial role. However, to the best of our knowledge, no research has systematically investigated prompt engineering in ASAG.\\n\\n, Bonthu et al. 2021, Putnikovic and Jovanovic 2023, Condor et al. 2021. \\n\\nASAG evaluates concise, open-ended responses against standard answers or specific criteria. ... Prompt Engineering for Automatic Short Answer Grading in Brazilian Portuguese ... Automatic Short Answer Grading (ASAG) is a prominent area of Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED). Despite much research, developing ASAG systems is challenging, even when focused on a single subject, mostly due to the variability in length and content of students' answers. While recent research has explored Large Language Models (LLMs) to enhance the efficiency of ASAG, the LLM performance is highly dependent on the prompt design. In that context, prompt engineering plays a crucial role. However, to the best of our knowledge, no research has systematically investigated prompt engineering in ASAG.\\n\\nThe proposed framework was rigorously tested on two benchmark datasets for Arabic short answer grading, AR-ASAG and Philosophy. \\n\\nOur findings indicate the crucial role of specific prompt components in improving GPT results and shows that GPT-4 consistently outperf\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/353824315_Automated_Short_Answer_Grading_Using_Deep_Learning_A_Survey\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"August 10, 2021\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Prompt Engineering for Automatic Short Answer Grading in Brazilian Portuguese | Scilit\", \"url\": \"https://www.scilit.com/publications/ec75d0e501a391c44e25eceba68262ae\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"scilit.com\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=scilit.com\", \"site_name\": \"Scilit\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Automatic Short Answer Grading (ASAG) is a prominent area of Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED). Despite much research, developing ASAG systems is challenging, even when focused on a single subject, mostly due to the variability in length and content of students&#x27; answers. While recent research has explored Large Language Models (LLMs) to enhance the efficiency of ASAG, the LLM performance is highly dependent on the prompt design. In that context, prompt engineering plays a crucial role. However, to the best of our knowledge, no research has systematically investigated prompt engineering in ASAG. Thus, this study compares over 128 prompt combinations for a Portuguese dataset based on GPT-3.5-Turbo and GPT-4-Turbo. Our findings indicate the crucial role of specific prompt components in improving GPT results and shows that GPT-4 consistently outperformed GPT-3.5 in this domain.\\n\\n\\n\\nAutomatic Short Answer Grading (ASAG) is a prominent area of Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED). Despite much research, developing ASAG systems is challenging, even when focused on a single subject, mostly due to the variability in length and content of students' answers. While recent research has explored Large Language Models (LLMs) to enhance the efficiency of ASAG, the LLM performance is highly dependent on the prompt design. In that context, prompt engineering plays a crucial role. However, to the best of our knowledge, no research has systematically investigated prompt engineering in ASAG. Thus, this study compares over 128 prompt combinations for a Portuguese dataset based on GPT-3.5-Turbo and GPT-4-Turbo. Our findings indicate the crucial role of specific prompt components in improving GPT results and shows that GPT-4 consistently outperformed GPT-3.5 in this domain.\\n\\nThese insights guide prompt design for ASAG in the context of Brazilian Portuguese. \\n\\nTherefore, we recommend students, educators, and developers leverage these findings to optimize prompt design a\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.scilit.com/publications/ec75d0e501a391c44e25eceba68262ae\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"(PDF) Portuguese Automatic Short Answer Grading\", \"url\": \"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/328735284_Portuguese_Automatic_Short_Answer_Grading\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"researchgate.net\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=researchgate.net\", \"site_name\": \"ResearchGate\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Six algorithms were used in this work: Leacock & Chodorow, Wu & Palmer, Lin, Resnik, Jiang & Conrath and Shortest Path.\\n\\nShort answers are routinely used in learning environments for stu-dents' assessment. Despite its importance, teachers find the task of assessing discursive answers very time-consuming. Aiming at assisting in this problem, this work explores the Automatic Short Answer Grading (ASAG) field using a machine learning approach. The literature was reviewed and 44 papers using different techniques were analyzed considering many aspects. A Portuguese dataset was build with more than 7000 short answers. Different approaches were experimented and a final model was created with their combination. The model's effectiveness showed to be satisfactory, with kappa scores indicating moderate/substantial agreement between the model and human grading. ... ... A grande parte dos estudos e do desenvolvimento na\\u00e1rea de ASAG concentra-se no idioma ingl\\u00eas [Burrows et al.\\n\\n\\n\\nA Corre\\u00e7\\u00e3o Autom\\u00e1tica de Respostas Curtas (ASAG) busca reduzir o esfor\\u00e7o humano em avalia\\u00e7\\u00f5es educacionais de larga escala, mas ainda h\\u00e1 poucas investiga\\u00e7\\u00f5es em portugu\\u00eas brasileiro. Este estudo compara tr\\u00eas grandes modelos de linguagem (GPT-4o-mini, Sabiazinho-3 e Gemini 2.0-Flash) e analisa o impacto de sete elementos de engenharia de prompt no desempenho dos modelos. Com base em um conjunto de dados em portugu\\u00eas, avaliamos todas as combina\\u00e7\\u00f5es poss\\u00edveis desses elementos. A combina\\u00e7\\u00e3o de exemplos few-shot com rubrica expl\\u00edcita foi a mais eficaz; o racioc\\u00ednio passo a passo beneficiou especialmente o GPT-4o-mini. Sabiazinho-3 teve maior concord\\u00e2ncia com humanos, Gemini 2.0-Flash obteve menor erro m\\u00e9dio absoluto, mas com mais alucina\\u00e7\\u00f5es, e o GPT-4o-mini gerou sa\\u00eddas num\\u00e9ricas mais limpas. ... ... \\n\\nIn the running example, we would expect three clusters of correct responses ({judiciary, judicary, \\u2026}, {legislative, legislation, \\u2026}, {executive branch, excutive, \\u2026}), and ad\", \"is_citable\": true, \"prompt_context_metadata\": {\"url\": \"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/328735284_Portuguese_Automatic_Short_Answer_Grading\", \"search_provider\": \"anthropic\", \"age\": \"November 16, 2018\"}}, {\"type\": \"knowledge\", \"title\": \"Automatic Grading of Portuguese Short Answers Using a Machine Learning Approach\", \"url\": \"https://sol.sbc.org.br/index.php/sbsi_estendido/article/download/13133/12986/\", \"metadata\": {\"type\": \"webpage_metadata\", \"site_domain\": \"sbc.org.br\", \"favicon_url\": \"https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=sbc.org.br\", \"site_name\": \"Sociedade Brasileira de Computa\\u00e7\\u00e3o\"}, \"is_missing\": false, \"text\": \"Automatic Grading of Portuguese Short Answers Using a \\u00b7 Machine Learning Approach\\n\\nASAG [Liu et al. 2016] and in other domains and applications as well [Vanbelle 2016]. The weighted kappa statistic was created to account for different levels of dis- agreement [Cohen 1968]. So, if one answer is given 1 by one grader and 2 by another, the disagreement is not as weighty as if the grades were 1 and 3 (in the \\ufb01rst case there is \\u00b7 a 1-point disagreement and in the second case there is a 2-point disagreement). Weights assigned to the kappa statistic are mainly linear or quadratic. The differ- ence between them is that in the linear scheme the disagreement from 0 to 1 and from 1 to \\u00b7 2 is equally weighted, which is not the case for the quadratic approach, where the higher \\u00b7 the two different scores, higher the penalty. It is recommended to report both weighting ... Table 2. Disagreement between raters ... The features are \\u00b7 extracted considering the similarity between students\\u2019 and references\\u2019 answers. This type \\u00b7 of similarity is widely employed in ASAG research [Burrows et al. 2015]. In the lexical level, similarity is given by how related words appears to be, based \\u00b7 on their constituents (letters). That means that two words that are very similar in shape \\u00b7 (or even equals: homonyms) can be get a high similarity score, even if the meaning is not \\u00b7 related (e.g. cell and cell phone). This problem is considered in this work and covered by ... Compression Distance. Four different variations were considered, depending on ... When searching for Portuguese ASAG systems, seven researches were found. They can \\u00b7 be divided into three groups. The \\ufb01rst is by works that employed manually crafted lin- guistic, syntactic or fuzzy rules to match student answers to the expected content. This \\u00b7 kind of automatic grading does not need a large amount of data, but the grading system is \\u00b7 restricted to the data. They worked with 1, 5 and 30 questions and 68, 15 and 300 student\\u2019s \\u00b7 answers in total, respectively [Salton et al. 2013, Vilela et al. 2012, Flores et al. 2014]. The second group of works consists of those who used ngrams and simil- arity metrics (with expected answers) to grade students answers.\\n\\nASAG research [Dzikovska et al. 2013, Sultan et al. \\n\\nsearch on the ASAG \\ufb01eld beyond their original authors and competitors as between \\u00b7 2014 and 2017 new works were developed using these datasets (e.g. [Higgins 2014, Ramachandran et al. 2015, Roy et al. 2016, Riordan et al. 2017], just to name a few). ... \"019e318a-4283-7836-9f5c-b1017a049bdc\", \"text\": \"V-20260516-031 \\u2014 Claude Export Benchmark Runner \\u2014 #ai #data \\u2014 Active\\n\\nID\\tChat Name\\tType\\tFrame\\tQuestion\\tOutput\\tData/Metric\\tOwner\\tPriority\\tStatus\\tTags\\nT-001\\tClaude Export Benchmark\\tTask\\tWorkflow\\tComo rodar nas contas restantes?\\t.py repeti\\u00e7\\u00e3o\\tLeonardo\\tHigh\\tActive\\t#risk\\n\\nCriei o .py pronto:\\n\\nDownload \\ufffcclaude_export_benchmark.py\\ufffc\\n\\nO que ele faz\\n\\nCamada\\tM\\u00e9trica\\tPara qu\\u00ea\\nNormaliza\\u00e7\\u00e3o\\tnormalized_messages.jsonl\\tUne batches/contas em formato limpo\\nConversas\\tconversations_metrics.csv\\tM\\u00e9tricas por conversa\\nConta\\taccount_summary.json/csv\\tResumo compar\\u00e1vel entre contas\\n\\u00cdndice\\tproductivity_master_index.md\\tMaster index com top conversas, tags, slash commands\\nSupabase\\tsupabase_schema.sql + supabase_upsert.sql\\tPrepara\\u00e7\\u00e3o opcional para inserir no banco\\nCompara\\u00e7\\u00e3o\\taccounts_comparison.csv/md\\tCompara duas ou mais contas\\n\\nA lente 4D est\\u00e1 alinhada ao curso da Anthropic Academy, que define AI Fluency como colabora\\u00e7\\u00e3o efetiva, eficiente, \\u00e9tica e segura com IA, e organiza o curr\\u00edculo em Delegation, Description, Discernment e Diligence. Usei isso como proxy operacional, n\\u00e3o como \\u201cm\\u00e9trica oficial de performance\\u201d.  \\ufffc\\n\\nRodar em uma conta/batch\\n\\npython claude_export_benchmark.py \\\\\\n  --input batch-0000.zip batch-0001.zip batch-0002.zip \\\\\\n  --account conta_maia_01 \\\\\\n  --out out/conta_maia_01 \\\\\\n  --emit-sql\\n\\nRodar em uma pasta com todos os zips restantes\\n\\npython claude_export_benchmark.py \\\\\\n  --input \\\"exports_conta_03/*.zip\\\" \\\\\\n  --account conta_03 \\\\\\n  --out out/conta_03 \\\\\\n  --emit-sql\\n\\nComparar contas\\n\\npython claude_export_benchmark.py \\\\\\n  --compare \\\\\\n  out/conta_maia_01/account_summary.json \\\\\\n  out/conta_maia_02/account_summary.json \\\\\\n  out/conta_03/account_summary.json \\\\\\n  --out out/comparativo_geral\\n\\nM\\u00e9trica-padr\\u00e3o adicionada\\n\\nScore\\tInterpreta\\u00e7\\u00e3o\\nproductivity_score\\tScore composto: 4D + estrutura + documento + CMD + inten\\u00e7\\u00e3o de entrega \\u2212 desperd\\u00edcio\\ndocument_ratio_score\\tMede se a conversa virou documento/workbench real\\ncontext_waste_score\\tPenaliza conversa sem nome, mensagem vazia e prompt repetido\\nslash_commands_per_100_conversations\\tMede maturidade de sistema reutiliz\\u00e1vel\\nnamed_conversation_pct\\tMede recuperabilidade do conhecimento\\ntext_coverage_pct\\tDetecta batch incompleto ou export ruim\\n\\nVeredicto operacional\\n\\nSeu problema n\\u00e3o \\u00e9 \\u201cuso ruim de IA\\u201d. \\u00c9 uso avan\\u00e7ado com baixa governan\\u00e7a de arquivo.\\n\\nA m\\u00e9trica que mais importa agora \\u00e9:\\n\\nprodutividade real = output estruturado recuper\\u00e1vel / contexto desperdi\\u00e7ado\\n\\nO .py foi desenhado para revelar exatamente isso nas Let me run the benchmark script on all of them.\\n```\\nThis block is not supported on your current device yet.\\n```\\n\\n\\n```\\nThis block is not supported on your current device yet.\\n```\\n\\n\\n```\\nThis block is not supported on your current device yet.\\n```\\n\\n\\n```\\nThis block is not supported on your current device yet.\\n```\\n\\n\\n```\\nThis block is not supported on your current device yet.\\n```\\n\\n\\n```\\nThis block is not supported on your current device yet.\\n```\\n\\n\\n```\\nThis block is not supported on your current device yet.\\n```\\n\\nRodou. 5 contas processadas, comparativo gerado. Aqui est\\u00e1 o diagn\\u00f3stico completo:\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Resultado \\u2014 Comparativo das 5 contas\\n\\n| M\\u00e9trica | **conta_365** | **lknhz** | **verinhaburgos** | directdiverg | lpab |\\n|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|\\n| Conversas | 13 | 61 | 13 | 147 | 236 |\\n| **Productivity Score** | **46.82** | **39.26** | **38.20** | 7.61 | 4.03 |\\n| **4D Score** | **41.48** | **34.10** | **38.79** | 20.52 | 18.09 |\\n| **Context Waste** | **3.61** | **14.25** | **16.50** | 73.04 | 78.15 |\\n| Named conversations % | 100% | 85% | 85% | 14% | 8% |\\n| Text coverage % | 94% | 80% | 65% | 11% | 11% |\\n| Doc-heavy % | 31% | 16% | 15% | 6% | 2% |\\n| Deliverable intent % | 62% | 61% | 46% | 9% | 6% |\\n| Avg prompt (chars) | 475 | 746 | 2034 | 692 | **30** |\\n| Slash cmds/100 convs | 254 | **298** | 231 | 135 | 64 |\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Leitura operacional\\n\\n**Tier A \\u2014 Uso estruturado**\\n- `conta_365`: menor volume, maior qualidade. 100% das conversas nomeadas, waste quase zero. Uso focado e orientado a entreg\\u00e1vel.\\n- `lknhz`: boa densidade de slash commands (298/100), deliverable intent alto (61%). Escala com governan\\u00e7a razo\\u00e1vel.\\n- `verinhaburgos`: prompts muito longos (2034 chars em m\\u00e9dia) \\u2014 sinal de contexto rico mas pode ser sobrecarga. Bom 4D.\\n\\n**Tier C \\u2014 Ru\\u00eddo alto**\\n- `directdiverg` e `lpab`: volume alto, governan\\u00e7a baixa. `lpab` tem 236 conversas com prompt m\\u00e9dio de **30 chars** \\u2014 uso fragmentado, provavelmente mobile/reflexivo, n\\u00e3o orientado a output. Context waste de 78% \\u00e9 o maior sinal de alerta.\\n\\n---\\n\\n**O que fazer agora com `directdiverg` e `lpab`:**\\nRodar mais batches vai confirmar o padr\\u00e3o \\u2014 mas o diagn\\u00f3stico j\\u00e1 \\u00e9 claro. O problema n\\u00e3o \\u00e9 quantidade de uso, \\u00e9 aus\\u00eancia de inten\\u00e7\\u00e3o estruturada. Naming + deliverable intent s\\u00e3o os dois alavancas mais f\\u00e1ceis de mover primeiro.\\n\\nO ZIP cont\\u00e9m todos os outputs por conta (`conversations_metrics.csv`, `productivity_master_index.md`, SQLs) + `accounts_comparison.csv/md`.\", \"content\": [{\"start_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T16:06:48.013668Z\", \"stop_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T16:06:48.013668Z\", \"flags\": null, \"type\": \"text\", \"text\": \" \", \"citations\": []}, {\"start_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T16:06:48.447308Z\", \"stop_timestamp\": \"2026-05-16T16:06:50.600374Z\", \"flags\": null, \"type\": \"thinking\", \"thinking\": \"The user has uploaded a Python file and a zip file. 138\\t\\n   139\\tdef safe_mkdir(path: Path) -> None:\\n   140\\t    path.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\\n   141\\t\\n   142\\t\\n   143\\tdef parse_dt(value: Any) -> str:\\n   144\\t    if not value:\\n   145\\t        return \\\"\\\"\\n   146\\t    if isinstance(value, (int, float)):\\n   147\\t        try:\\n   148\\t            # Claude exports may use milliseconds or seconds.\\n   149\\t            ts = value / 1000 if value > 10_000_000_000 else value\\n   150\\t            return datetime.fromtimestamp(ts, tz=timezone.utc).isoformat()\\n   151\\t        except Exception:\\n   152\\t            return str(value)\\n   153\\t    if isinstance(value, str):\\n   154\\t        s = value.strip()\\n   155\\t        # Normalize common Z suffix.\\n   156\\t        if s.endswith(\\\"Z\\\"):\\n   157\\t            s = s[:-1] + \\\"+00:00\\\"\\n   158\\t        try:\\n   159\\t            return datetime.fromisoformat(s).isoformat()\\n   160\\t        except
